abstract:
E-commerce recommendation systems aim to generate ordered lists of items for
customers, optimizing multiple business objectives, such as clicks, conversions
and Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV). Traditional multi-objective optimization
methods like formulas or Learning-to-rank (LTR) models take effect at
item-level, neglecting dynamic user intent and contextual item interactions.
List-level multi-objective optimization in the re-ranking stage can overcome
this limitation, but most current re-ranking models focus more on accuracy
improvement with context. In addition, re-ranking is faced with the challenges
of time complexity and diversity. In light of this, we propose a novel
end-to-end generative re-ranking model named Sequential Ordered Regression
Transformer-Generator (SORT-Gen) for the less-studied list-level
multi-objective optimization problem. Specifically, SORT-Gen is divided into
two parts: 1)Sequential Ordered Regression Transformer innovatively uses
Transformer and ordered regression to accurately estimate multi-objective
values for variable-length sub-lists. 2)Mask-Driven Fast Generation Algorithm
combines multi-objective candidate queues, efficient item selection and
diversity mechanism into model inference, providing a fast online list
generation method. Comprehensive online experiments demonstrate that SORT-Gen
brings +4.13% CLCK and +8.10% GMV for Baiyibutie, a notable Mini-app of Taobao.
Currently, SORT-Gen has been successfully deployed in multiple scenarios of
Taobao App, serving for a vast number of users.

keyInsights: Re-framing re-ranking as an end-to-end generative task, leveraging Transformer architectures for sequence generation.
Introducing Ordered Regression Loss to effectively model the cumulative, incremental value of multiple objectives for variable-length sub-lists.
Developing a mask-driven, single forward pass generation algorithm that efficiently overcomes combinatorial complexity and directly integrates diversity (MMR).
originalProblem: Traditional item-level multi-objective optimization (MOO) methods fail to adapt to dynamic user intent within a session and neglect contextual item interactions within a recommended list.
Re-ranking faces computational challenges due to combinatorial complexity (O(N!)) for generating optimal lists and often struggles to maintain diversity in recommendations.
A lack of comprehensive model-driven solutions for joint list-level multi-objective optimization, which is crucial for balancing conflicting business metrics in e-commerce.
results: Achieved +4.13% increase in Clicks (CLCK) and +8.10% increase in Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV) in online A/B tests on a Taobao mini-app compared to a strong baseline.
Demonstrated an end-to-end latency of 19 ms, proving its practical viability for real-time deployment in large-scale e-commerce systems.
Ablation studies confirmed the superiority of the Ordered Regression Loss and multi-objective candidate queues, with integrated MMR crucial for maintaining diversity and balanced performance.
solution: Proposes SORT-Gen, a novel generative re-ranking model for joint list-level multi-objective optimization, composed of the Sequential Ordered Regression Transformer (SORT) and a Mask-Driven Fast Generation Algorithm.
SORT utilizes a Transformer architecture with a causal mask and an innovative Ordered Regression Loss to accurately estimate cumulative multi-objective values for variable-length sub-lists.

The Mask-Driven Fast Generation Algorithm efficiently generates the entire ordered list in a single forward pass using multi-objective candidate queues and integrates Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) for diversity.
paper_summary.summary: SORT-Gen, a generative re-ranking model developed by Alibaba's Taobao & Tmall Group, addresses list-level multi-objective optimization by generating entire item lists. It achieved a +4.13% increase in clicks and +8.10% in GMV in online A/B tests on Taobao, with 19 ms latency, demonstrating a powerful and efficient solution for optimizing multiple business objectives simultaneously.

title: A Generative Re-ranking Model for List-level Multi-objective  Optimization at Taobao

abstract:
Generative recommendation has recently emerged as a promising paradigm in
information retrieval. However, generative ranking systems are still
understudied, particularly with respect to their effectiveness and feasibility
in large-scale industrial settings. This paper investigates this topic at the
ranking stage of Xiaohongshu's Explore Feed, a recommender system that serves
hundreds of millions of users. Specifically, we first examine how generative
ranking outperforms current industrial recommenders. Through theoretical and
empirical analyses, we find that the primary improvement in effectiveness stems
from the generative architecture, rather than the training paradigm. To
facilitate efficient deployment of generative ranking, we introduce GenRank, a
novel generative architecture for ranking. We validate the effectiveness and
efficiency of our solution through online A/B experiments. The results show
that GenRank achieves significant improvements in user satisfaction with nearly
equivalent computational resources compared to the existing production system.

keyInsights: The generative architecture itself, particularly its auto-regressive sequential interaction manner, is the primary driver of effectiveness, rather than grouped training sample organization.
Content embeddings yield over twice the AUC improvement under the generative paradigm compared to conventional models, due to architectural consistency.
While generative architectures reduce the overall need for extensive feature engineering, real-time window-based statistical features remain remarkably effective in providing direct signals.
originalProblem: Generative recommendation, while promising, faced challenges with computational intensity and scalability for large-scale industrial ranking systems serving hundreds of millions of users.
Existing generative architectures struggled to meet the stringent efficiency requirements of production environments, making their deployment difficult.
There was a lack of understanding regarding the specific mechanisms and architectural choices that drive the effectiveness of generative recommendation.
results: GenRank achieved a 94.8% training speed-up compared to the baseline generative model (HSTU) while maintaining or slightly improving offline AUC.
Online A/B tests on Xiaohongshu showed GenRank improved Time Spent by +0.33%, Number of Reads by +0.63%, Number of Engagements by +1.25%, and LT7 by +0.15%.

GenRank demonstrated comparable overall resource overhead to the existing production ranking model, with higher training costs but lower inference and storage costs, and a P99 response time improvement of over 25%.
solution: Proposed GenRank, a novel generative ranking architecture optimized for large-scale industrial deployment by focusing on efficiency.
Introduced an 'action-oriented organization' for input sequences, which halves sequence length to significantly reduce attention and linear projection costs.
Designed linear I/O position and time biases, complemented by a parameter-free ALiBi bias, to address the computational bottleneck of quadratic relative attention biases.
paper_summary.summary: Xiaohongshu Inc. developed GenRank, an efficient generative ranking architecture, to investigate and deploy generative recommendation in large-scale industrial settings. The system improved online user satisfaction metrics by up to 1.25% in A/B tests and achieved over 25% better P99 response time compared to the production model.

title: Towards Large-scale Generative Ranking

abstract: Generative retrieval has recently emerged as a promising approach to sequential recommendation, framing candidate item retrieval as an autoregressive sequence generation problem. However, existing generative methods typically focus solely on either behavioral or semantic aspects of item information, neglecting their complementary nature and thus resulting in limited effectiveness. To address this limitation, we introduce EAGER, a novel generative recommendation framework that seamlessly integrates both behavioral and semantic information. Specifically, we identify three key challenges in combining these two types of information: a unified generative architecture capable of handling two feature types, ensuring sufficient and independent learning for each type, and fostering subtle interactions that enhance collaborative information utilization. To achieve these goals, we propose (1) a two-stream generation architecture leveraging a shared encoder and two separate decoders to decode behavior tokens and semantic tokens with a confidence-based ranking strategy; (2) a global contrastive task with summary tokens to achieve discriminative decoding for each type of information; and (3) a semantic-guided transfer task designed to implicitly promote cross-interactions through reconstruction and estimation objectives. We validate the effectiveness of EAGER on four public benchmarks, demonstrating its superior performance compared to existing methods.

keyInsights: Behavioral and semantic information are complementary and can be effectively integrated within a unified generative framework for improved sequential recommendation.

Separating generative decoders for each data stream while enabling global knowledge distillation and subtle cross-stream transfer (rather than direct feature fusion) is crucial for heterogeneous data collaboration.

Framing item retrieval as an end-to-end autoregressive generation of item codes allows for holistic optimization, bypassing limitations of traditional two-stage approaches that separate representation learning from retrieval.
originalProblem: Traditional recommender systems often employ a two-stage paradigm (representation learning then ANN search), leading to suboptimal end-to-end optimization.
Existing generative recommenders are typically 'uni-stream,' relying solely on either behavioral or semantic item information, neglecting their complementary nature and missing out on rich insights.

Integrating heterogeneous behavioral and semantic data into a single generative framework presents challenges in ensuring discriminative learning, preserving distinct value, and enabling subtle, effective interaction between streams.
results: EAGER consistently outperforms all categories of baselines, including state-of-the-art generative models like TIGER, across four public datasets (Amazon Beauty, Sports and Outdoors, Toys and Games, Yelp).
It achieves up to 31.49% higher Recall@5 and 32.26% higher NDCG@5 compared to TIGER on the Beauty dataset, and 20.45% better Recall@5 on the Yelp dataset.

Ablation studies confirm that all proposed modules (Two-stream Generation Architecture, Global Contrastive Task, Semantic-guided Transfer Task) are essential, with the Two-stream Generation Architecture contributing most significantly to performance gains.
solution: EAGER proposes a Two-Stream Generation Architecture (TSG) with a shared encoder and dual Transformer decoders for independent generation of behavioral and semantic item codes.
A Global Contrastive Task (GCT) distills discriminative global knowledge from pre-trained item embeddings into generated code sequences using a novel summary token and positive-only contrastive loss.

A Semantic-guided Transfer Task (STT) facilitates subtle, implicit knowledge exchange from semantic to behavioral streams via an auxiliary bidirectional Transformer decoder trained with reconstruction and recognition objectives.
paper_summary.summary: EAGER, developed by Zhejiang University and Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, introduces a two-stream generative recommender system that integrates behavioral and semantic information through a unique architecture for enhanced personalization. This framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art generative baselines, achieving up to 31.49% higher Recall@5 on benchmark datasets.

title: EAGER: Two-Stream Generative Recommender with Behavior-Semantic Collaboration

abstract:
Reranking models solve the final recommendation lists that best fulfill
users' demands. While existing solutions focus on finding parametric models
that approximate optimal policies, recent approaches find that it is better to
generate multiple lists to compete for a ``pass'' ticket from an evaluator,
where the evaluator serves as the supervisor who accurately estimates the
performance of the candidate lists. In this work, we show that we can achieve a
more efficient and effective list proposal with a multi-generator framework and
provide empirical evidence on two public datasets and online A/B tests. More
importantly, we verify that the effectiveness of a generator is closely related
to how much it complements the views of other generators with sufficiently
different rerankings, which derives the metric of list comprehensiveness. With
this intuition, we design an automatic complementary generator-finding
framework that learns a policy that simultaneously aligns the users'
preferences and maximizes the list comprehensiveness metric. The experimental
results indicate that the proposed framework can further improve the
multi-generator reranking performance.

keyInsights: Multiple complementary generators perform better than single generators due to ensemble effects
List diversity correlates positively with reranking performance
Automated generator selection can replace manual expert design
originalProblem: Existing generator-only and generator-evaluator approaches struggle with combinatorial complexity or inefficient exploration
Independent generators often produce similar rankings, reducing the effectiveness of multi-generator systems
results: Outperformed single-generator baselines in offline tests on Avito and RecFlow datasets
Improved watch time, effective views, and social interactions in live A/B tests on Kuaishou platform
Successfully demonstrated CLIG's effectiveness across different generator architectures
Achieved better recommendation diversity while maintaining accuracy compared to existing approaches
solution: Multi-Generator-Evaluator framework that leverages multiple diverse generators before evaluation
Complementary List Generation (CLIG) technique that automatically learns generators to maximize both user preferences and list diversity
List Comprehensiveness metric to evaluate and guide generator selection
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Kuaishou Technology develop a Multi-Generator-Evaluator (MG-E) framework for recommendation reranking that combines multiple list generators with an evaluator, introducing a List Comprehensiveness metric and Complementary List Generation technique to improve recommendation diversity and accuracy across both offline benchmarks and live A/B tests.

title: Comprehensive List Generation for Multi-Generator Reranking

abstract:
Generative models have recently gained attention in recommendation systems by
directly predicting item identifiers from user interaction sequences. However,
existing methods suffer from significant information loss due to the separation
of stages such as quantization and sequence modeling, hindering their ability
to achieve the modeling precision and accuracy of sequential dense retrieval
techniques. Integrating generative and dense retrieval methods remains a
critical challenge. To address this, we introduce the Cascaded Organized
Bi-Represented generAtive retrieval (COBRA) framework, which innovatively
integrates sparse semantic IDs and dense vectors through a cascading process.
Our method alternates between generating these representations by first
generating sparse IDs, which serve as conditions to aid in the generation of
dense vectors. End-to-end training enables dynamic refinement of dense
representations, capturing both semantic insights and collaborative signals
from user-item interactions. During inference, COBRA employs a coarse-to-fine
strategy, starting with sparse ID generation and refining them into dense
vectors via the generative model. We further propose BeamFusion, an innovative
approach combining beam search with nearest neighbor scores to enhance
inference flexibility and recommendation diversity. Extensive experiments on
public datasets and offline tests validate our method's robustness. Online A/B
tests on a real-world advertising platform with over 200 million daily users
demonstrate substantial improvements in key metrics, highlighting COBRA's
practical advantages.

author_info.institution: Beijing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

keyInsights: A cascaded representation unifying sparse semantic IDs and dense vectors can overcome information loss in generative recommendation by capturing both categorical and fine-grained item details.
The BeamFusion inference technique, which combines beam search with nearest neighbor retrieval, allows for a flexible trade-off between recommendation precision and diversity.
Joint end-to-end training of the sparse ID generation, dense vector learning, and sequential prediction components is crucial for optimizing overall recommendation performance.
originalProblem: Generative recommendation models, which often rely on sparse semantic IDs, suffer from information loss during item representation, leading to less accurate recommendations.
Existing generative approaches struggle to capture the fine-grained similarity between items that dense retrieval methods excel at.
Combining the efficiency and flexibility of generative models with the fine-grained accuracy of dense retrieval methods remains a challenge without inheriting their respective weaknesses.
results: COBRA consistently outperformed state-of-the-art sequential recommendation methods and generative models on public benchmark datasets, showing improvements in Recall@K and NDCG@K.
Online A/B tests on Baidu's advertising platform demonstrated substantial real-world gains, including a 3.8% increase in conversion rate and a 2.7% increase in Average Revenue Per User (ARPU).
Ablation studies confirmed that both sparse IDs and dense vectors are essential for COBRA's performance, with each component contributing uniquely to accuracy and diversity.
solution: COBRA introduces a cascaded sparse-dense representation where each item is represented by both a sparse semantic ID (from a quantized VAE) and a dense vector (from a bidirectional Transformer encoder).
A Transformer decoder processes these cascaded representations to model sequential user-item interactions and predict the next item in a two-stage process (first sparse ID, then dense vector).

A novel inference technique called BeamFusion combines beam search for sparse ID generation with nearest neighbor search based on dense vectors, allowing for controlled precision-diversity trade-off in recommendations.
paper_summary.summary: COBRA (Cascaded sparsE-dense RepresentAtion) is a framework that combines sparse IDs with dense vectors for generative recommendation, improving recommendation performance and offering control over diversity. The approach achieved a 3.8% increase in conversion rate and a 2.7% increase in Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) in online A/B tests on a large-scale industrial platform.

title: Sparse Meets Dense: Unified Generative Recommendations with Cascaded  Sparse-Dense Representations

abstract:
Modern industrial advertising systems commonly employ Multi-stage Cascading
Architectures (MCA) to balance computational efficiency with ranking accuracy.
However, this approach presents two fundamental challenges: (1) performance
inconsistencies arising from divergent optimization targets and capability
differences between stages, and (2) failure to account for advertisement
externalities - the complex interactions between candidate ads during ranking.
These limitations ultimately compromise system effectiveness and reduce
platform profitability. In this paper, we present EGA-V1, an end-to-end
generative architecture that unifies online advertising ranking as one model.
EGA-V1 replaces cascaded stages with a single model to directly generate
optimal ad sequences from the full candidate ad corpus in location-based
services (LBS). The primary challenges associated with this approach stem from
high costs of feature processing and computational bottlenecks in modeling
externalities of large-scale candidate pools. To address these challenges,
EGA-V1 introduces an algorithm and engine co-designed hybrid feature service to
decouple user and ad feature processing, reducing latency while preserving
expressiveness. To efficiently extract intra- and cross-sequence mutual
information, we propose RecFormer with an innovative cluster-attention
mechanism as its core architectural component. Furthermore, we propose a
bi-stage training strategy that integrates pre-training with reinforcement
learning-based post-training to meet sophisticated platform and advertising
objectives. Extensive offline evaluations on public benchmarks and large-scale
online A/B testing on industrial advertising platform have demonstrated the
superior performance of EGA-V1 over state-of-the-art MCAs.

keyInsights: A unified end-to-end generative model can effectively resolve the objective misalignments and prediction inconsistencies inherent in multi-stage cascading architectures.

Efficient and comprehensive modeling of interactions among a large pool of candidate advertisements (externalities) is achievable at industrial scale through innovative attention mechanisms like cluster-attention.

Economic constraints crucial for online advertising, such as auction dynamics, bid optimization, and incentive compatibility, can be successfully integrated and learned within an end-to-end framework using specialized components and reinforcement learning strategies.
originalProblem: Traditional Multi-stage Cascading Architectures (MCAs) in online advertising suffer from performance inconsistencies due to divergent optimization targets and capacities across sequential stages.

Existing ranking approaches often fail to model complex mutual influences and interactions (externalities) among candidate advertisements, operating under an independent click-through rate assumption.
Processing massive, high-dimensional features and complex interactions in large-scale advertising systems incurs significant computational bottlenecks, storage, transmission overhead, and latency.
results: Offline experiments on an industrial dataset showed EGA-V1 achieved a +20.4% lift in Recall@50 and +1.48% AUC compared to the strongest baseline.
EGA-V1 increased expected CTR by +8.3% and expected RPM by +11.4% offline, significantly reducing the Incentive Compatibility Metric (Ψ) to 2.3% from over 9% for baselines.
Online A/B tests at Meituan demonstrated a +5.2% improvement in CTR, a +13.6% increase in RPM, and a +3.1% gain in advertiser ROI, with only a +2.2% (5ms) increase in online response time.
solution: EGA-V1 introduces a unified end-to-end generative architecture composed of a Hybrid Feature Service (HFS), RecFormer, and AucFormer to integrate ad ranking and allocation.

The Hybrid Feature Service (HFS) optimizes feature processing by categorizing feature storage, leveraging local storage for ad embeddings, and implicitly recovering cross-features via neural interactions.

RecFormer employs a Global Cluster-Former (GCF) and Mid-fusion Interest-Former (MIF) with an efficient O(N 

Nc 

d) cluster-attention mechanism to model deep user interests and candidate externalities over large ad sets.

AucFormer integrates auction dynamics through a non-autoregressive generator, a permutation-aware evaluator, and a payment network to optimize for profitability while satisfying economic constraints like incentive compatibility and individual rationality.
paper_summary.summary: EGA-V1 presents an end-to-end generative architecture that unifies the online advertising ranking and allocation pipeline into a single model, addressing limitations of traditional multi-stage systems. Deployed at Meituan, it achieved a +5.2% CTR, +13.6% RPM, and +3.1% ROI in online A/B tests, while incurring a minimal +2.2% increase in online response time.

title: EGA-V1: Unifying Online Advertising with End-to-End Learning

abstract:
In recommendation systems, the traditional multi-stage paradigm, which
includes retrieval and ranking, often suffers from information loss between
stages and diminishes performance. Recent advances in generative models,
inspired by natural language processing, suggest the potential for unifying
these stages to mitigate such loss. This paper presents the Unified Generative
Recommendation Framework (UniGRF), a novel approach that integrates retrieval
and ranking into a single generative model. By treating both stages as sequence
generation tasks, UniGRF enables sufficient information sharing without
additional computational costs, while remaining model-agnostic. To enhance
inter-stage collaboration, UniGRF introduces a ranking-driven enhancer module
that leverages the precision of the ranking stage to refine retrieval
processes, creating an enhancement loop. Besides, a gradient-guided adaptive
weighter is incorporated to dynamically balance the optimization of retrieval
and ranking, ensuring synchronized performance improvements. Extensive
experiments demonstrate that UniGRF significantly outperforms existing models
on benchmark datasets, confirming its effectiveness in facilitating information
transfer. Ablation studies and further experiments reveal that UniGRF not only
promotes efficient collaboration between stages but also achieves synchronized
optimization. UniGRF provides an effective, scalable, and compatible framework
for generative recommendation systems.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Treating recommendation as a unified sequence generation task enables better information sharing
High-precision ranking feedback can be used to generate better training samples for retrieval
Synchronized optimization across stages is crucial for optimal performance
originalProblem: Traditional multi-stage recommendation systems suffer from information loss between retrieval and ranking stages
Existing approaches handle retrieval and ranking separately, leading to sub-optimal performance and inefficient training
results: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on MovieLens-1M, MovieLens-20M and Amazon-Books datasets
Demonstrates improved performance scaling with model size following scaling laws
Ablation studies confirm effectiveness of ranking-driven enhancement and adaptive weighting components
Ranking stage benefits more significantly from unified approach compared to retrieval stage
solution: Unified generative framework that treats both retrieval and ranking as sequence generation tasks
Ranking-driven enhancer module that leverages ranking precision to improve retrieval training
Gradient-guided adaptive weighter that balances optimization speeds between stages
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from USTC and Huawei Noah's Ark Lab develop UniGRF, a unified generative framework that integrates recommendation retrieval and ranking into a single model through ranking-driven enhancement and gradient-guided adaptive weighting, demonstrating superior performance on multiple benchmarks while reducing information loss between stages compared to traditional multi-stage approaches.

title: Killing Two Birds with One Stone: Unifying Retrieval and Ranking with a  Single Generative Recommendation Model

abstract:
Reranking is a critical component in recommender systems, playing an
essential role in refining the output of recommendation algorithms. Traditional
reranking models have focused predominantly on accuracy, but modern
applications demand consideration of additional criteria such as diversity and
fairness. Existing reranking approaches often fail to harmonize these diverse
criteria effectively at the model level. Moreover, these models frequently
encounter challenges with scalability and personalization due to their
complexity and the varying significance of different reranking criteria in
diverse scenarios. In response, we introduce a comprehensive reranking
framework enhanced by LLM, designed to seamlessly integrate various reranking
criteria while maintaining scalability and facilitating personalized
recommendations. This framework employs a fully connected graph structure,
allowing the LLM to simultaneously consider multiple aspects such as accuracy,
diversity, and fairness through a coherent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process. A
customizable input mechanism is also integrated, enabling the tuning of the
language model's focus to meet specific reranking needs. We validate our
approach using three popular public datasets, where our framework demonstrates
superior performance over existing state-of-the-art reranking models in
balancing multiple criteria.

keyInsights: LLMs can effectively bridge the semantic gap between diverse reranking aspects by integrating them into a unified semantic space through a multi-hop Chain-of-Thought reasoning process.
A modular, graph-based architecture combined with customizable natural language goals enables dynamic, scalable, and personalized reranking strategies that adapt to evolving requirements.

The ability for the LLM to maintain a historical reranking pool and utilize reflective functional nodes (like "Backward") is crucial for making informed, strategic decisions and optimizing the reranking sequence.
originalProblem: Existing reranking models primarily focus on single objectives like accuracy, struggling to comprehensively consider and balance multiple diverse aspects (e.g., accuracy, diversity, fairness) simultaneously due to a substantial semantic gap.
Current models lack scalability to easily incorporate new aspects or custom reranking rules, limiting their flexibility to adapt to varied recommendation settings.
The output tendencies of deployed reranking models are typically fixed, preventing dynamic personalization based on evolving business objectives or individual user preferences.
results: LLM4Rerank consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art reranking models, including both traditional deep learning and other LLM-based baselines, across accuracy (HR, NDCG), diversity ($\alpha$-NDCG), and fairness (MAD) metrics on all evaluated datasets.

The framework demonstrates strong personalization, with the LLM's chosen reranking paths and node utilization rates accurately reflecting the aspect priorities specified in the natural language "Goal" sentences (e.g., higher Diversity node utilization when diversity is prioritized).

Ablation studies confirmed the critical importance of the historical reranking pool and the automatic reranking mechanism, with their removal leading to significant performance degradation across all evaluated metrics.
solution: LLM4Rerank employs a fully connected function graph with distinct aspect (accuracy, diversity, fairness) and functional (backward, stop) nodes to guide a zero-shot LLM through a multi-step reranking process.

A natural language "Goal" sentence allows users to dynamically define prioritized aspects, enabling the LLM to interpret semantic connections and automatically transition between nodes, forming a Chain-of-Thought reasoning path.
A historical reranking pool records outcomes from each node, providing context for subsequent decisions and allowing the LLM to reflect on previous choices, enhancing strategic depth.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from City University of Hong Kong and Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab developed LLM4Rerank, an automated framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) and a function graph to integrate and balance multiple reranking aspects, including accuracy, diversity, and fairness. This method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines on ML-1M, KuaiRand, and Douban-Movie datasets by dynamically adapting reranking strategies based on user-defined goals.

title: LLM4Rerank: LLM-based Auto-Reranking Framework for Recommendations

abstract: Recent studies increasingly explore Large Language Models (LLMs) as a new paradigm for recommendation systems due to their scalability and world knowledge. However, existing work has three key limitations: (1) most efforts focus on retrieval and ranking, while the reranking phase, critical for refining final recommendations, is largely overlooked; (2) LLMs are typically used in zero-shot or supervised fine-tuning settings, leaving their reasoning abilities, especially those enhanced through reinforcement learning (RL) and high-quality reasoning data, underexploited; (3) items are commonly represented by non-semantic IDs, creating major scalability challenges in industrial systems with billions of identifiers. To address these gaps, we propose the Generative Reasoning Reranker (GR2), an end-to-end framework with a three-stage training pipeline tailored for reranking. First, a pretrained LLM is mid-trained on semantic IDs encoded from non-semantic IDs via a tokenizer achieving $\ge$99% uniqueness. Next, a stronger larger-scale LLM generates high-quality reasoning traces through carefully designed prompting and rejection sampling, which are used for supervised fine-tuning to impart foundational reasoning skills. Finally, we apply Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization (DAPO), enabling scalable RL supervision with verifiable rewards designed specifically for reranking. Experiments on two real-world datasets demonstrate GR2's effectiveness: it surpasses the state-of-the-art OneRec-Think by 2.4% in Recall@5 and 1.3% in NDCG@5. Ablations confirm that advanced reasoning traces yield substantial gains across metrics. We further find that RL reward design is crucial in reranking: LLMs tend to exploit reward hacking by preserving item order, motivating conditional verifiable rewards to mitigate this behavior and optimize reranking performance.

keyInsights: Achieving high uniqueness (>99%) in Semantic IDs (SIDs) is crucial for scalability, with EMA updates and randomizing the last quantization level being essential techniques, while contrastive loss is vital for downstream recommendation quality.

Structured, hierarchical reasoning traces generated by a stronger "teacher" LLM, particularly through rejection sampling, significantly improve the "student" LLM's ability to interpret item semantics and user preferences.

Supervised fine-tuning for reasoning alone does not consistently translate into improved reranking performance, highlighting the necessity of reinforcement learning to explicitly optimize the LLM's reasoning process towards actual ranking objectives and mitigate "reward hacking."
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based recommendation systems primarily focus on initial retrieval and ranking, largely neglecting the critical reranking phase for final personalization and refinement.

Many LLM-RecSys approaches underutilize the advanced reasoning capabilities of LLMs, often employing them in basic fine-tuning settings without leveraging structured reasoning or reinforcement learning.
Industrial recommendation systems face scalability challenges with non-semantic item IDs, leading to immense vocabulary expansion and inefficient item representation for LLMs at scale.
results: GR2's enhanced tokenization and mid-training strategy yielded a significant 18.7% improvement in NDCG@5 over the OneRec-Think itemic alignment baseline on the Amazon Beauty dataset.

Reasoning traces generated via rejection sampling with hierarchical item information consistently outperformed vanilla traces, resulting in relative improvements of 3.30% in Recall@5 and 2.60% in NDCG@5.

The full GR2 framework consistently surpassed state-of-the-art baselines, including OneRec-Think, with overall improvements of 2.4% in Recall@5 and 1.3% in NDCG@5, confirming its effectiveness in LLM-based reranking.
solution: GR2 implements a three-stage training pipeline encompassing Tokenized Mid-Training for semantic item representation, Reasoning Data Generation and Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT), and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for policy optimization.

It converts non-semantic item identifiers into highly unique Semantic IDs (SIDs) using an RQ-VAE with techniques like EMA updates and randomizing the last quantization level, followed by mid-training to align SIDs with the LLM's world knowledge.

High-quality, hierarchical reasoning traces are generated by a powerful "teacher" LLM through meticulous prompt engineering and rejection sampling, which are then used to fine-tune a "student" LLM via SFT and further optimized with an adapted DAPO algorithm using a custom conditional reward function.
paper_summary.summary: The Generative Reasoning Re-ranker (GR2), developed by Meta AI, introduces a three-stage framework that integrates Large Language Models into the reranking phase of recommendation systems. GR2 utilizes semantic item identifiers, generates hierarchical reasoning traces, and employs reinforcement learning to achieve state-of-the-art performance on real-world datasets, surpassing previous LLM-RecSys methods by up to 2.4% in Recall@5 and 1.3% in NDCG@5.

title: Generative Reasoning Re-ranker

abstract: Slate recommendation, which presents users with a ranked item list in a single display, is ubiquitous across mainstream online platforms. Recent advances in generative models have shown significant potential for this task via autoregressive modeling of discrete semantic ID sequences. However, existing methods suffer from three key limitations: entangled item tokenization, inefficient sequential decoding, and the absence of holistic slate planning. These issues often result in substantial inference overhead and inadequate alignment with diverse user preferences and practical business requirements, hindering the industrial deployment of generative slate recommendation systems. In this paper, we propose HiGR, an efficient generative slate recommendation framework that integrates hierarchical planning with listwise preference alignment. First, we design an auto-encoder incorporating residual quantization and contrastive constraints, which tokenizes items into semantically structured IDs to enable controllable generation. Second, HiGR decouples the generation process into two stages: a list-level planning stage to capture global slate intent, and an item-level decoding stage to select specific items, effectively reducing the search space and enabling efficient generation. Third, we introduce a multi-objective and listwise preference alignment mechanism that enhances slate quality by leveraging implicit user feedback. Extensive experiments have validated the effectiveness of our HiGR method. Notably, it outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by over 10\% in offline recommendation quality while achieving a $5\times$ inference speedup. Furthermore, we have deployed HiGR on a commercial platform under Tencent (serving hundreds of millions of users), and online A/B tests show that it increases average watch time and average video plays by 1.22\% and 1.73\%, respectively.

keyInsights: Structuring item tokenization with prefix-level contrastive constraints effectively reduces semantic entanglement and enhances consistency.
A hierarchical decoding approach, separating coarse-grained slate planning from fine-grained item generation, significantly boosts inference efficiency.
Holistic, multi-objective preference alignment using ORPO on slate-level feedback improves overall recommendation quality by optimizing for diverse user preferences simultaneously.
originalProblem: Semantic entanglement in item tokenization makes precise control over generation difficult and compromises output consistency.
Inefficient sequential decoding in autoregressive models causes high inference latency, posing a challenge for real-time recommendation systems.
Lack of holistic slate planning in existing generative methods often results in locally coherent but globally suboptimal slates, failing to meet list-level objectives like diversity.
results: HiGR consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 0.3163 Impressions Hit@5 and 0.0831 NDCG@5 on a large industrial dataset.
The system demonstrated a 5x inference speedup compared to prior generative methods while delivering superior recommendation quality.
Online A/B tests on a commercial platform yielded relative gains of +1.22% in average watch time and +1.73% in average video views.
solution: A Contrastive Residual Quantized Variational Auto-Encoder (CRQ-VAE) creates semantically coherent and collaborative item tokens using prefix-level contrastive constraints.
A Hierarchical Slate Decoder (HSD) separates global slate planning from fine-grained item generation, improving efficiency and coherence through a coarse-to-fine approach.

Multi-objective Preference Alignment, based on Odds Ratio Preference Optimization (ORPO), fine-tunes the model using slate-level feedback to optimize for ranking fidelity, user interest, and diversity.
paper_summary.summary: A generative framework for slate recommendation, HiGR, addresses challenges in tokenization and inference efficiency through hierarchical planning and multi-objective preference alignment. The system showed improved recommendation quality with a 5x inference speedup and led to online gains of +1.22% in average watch time on a large commercial media platform.

title: HiGR: Efficient Generative Slate Recommendation via Hierarchical Planning and Multi-Objective Preference Alignment

abstract: The end-to-end generative paradigm is revolutionizing advertising recommendation systems, driving a shift from traditional cascaded architectures towards unified modeling. However, practical deployment faces three core challenges: the misalignment between interest objectives and business value, the target-agnostic limitation of generative processes, and the disconnection between generation and ranking stages. Existing solutions often fall into a dilemma where single-stage fusion induces optimization tension, while stage decoupling causes irreversible information loss. To address this, we propose OneRanker, achieving architectural-level deep integration of generation and ranking. First, we design a value-aware multi-task decoupling architecture. By leveraging task token sequences and causal mask, we separate interest coverage and value optimization spaces within shared representations, effectively alleviating target conflicts. Second, we construct a coarse-to-fine collaborative target awareness mechanism, utilizing Fake Item Tokens for implicit awareness during generation and a ranking decoder for explicit value alignment at the candidate level. Finally, we propose input-output dual-side consistency guarantees. Through Key/Value pass-through mechanisms and Distribution Consistency (DC) Constraint Loss, we achieve end-to-end collaborative optimization between generation and ranking. The full deployment on Tencent's WeiXin channels advertising system has shown a significant improvement in key business metrics (GMV - Normal +1.34\%), providing a new paradigm with industrial feasibility for generative advertising recommendations.

keyInsights: Architectural integration of generation and ranking, utilizing task tokens for objective decoupling and 'fake item tokens' for dynamic item semantic awareness, effectively addresses objective misalignment and target-agnostic generation.

A novel heterogeneous attention decoder with cross-attention prioritization and a refined mask strategy enhances recommendation accuracy by dynamically perceiving item characteristics during generation.

A distributional consistency loss, acting as a soft label supervision from the ranker, provides backward gradient pathways to align the generator's policy with the ranker's calibrated scores, mitigating value biases and ensuring consistency.
originalProblem: Misalignment between user interest objectives and business value objectives in generative recommendation models, leading to optimization tension or early filtering of high-value candidates.
The 'target-agnostic' nature of generative processes, where user representations remain static and do not dynamically adapt to candidate item characteristics during generation.
Architectural disconnections in 'generate-then-rank' systems, causing representation inconsistency, computational redundancy, and error propagation between stages.
results: Offline experiments showed OneRanker significantly outperformed baselines, with a 44.7% relative improvement in HR@1 (0.2639 vs. 0.1824) and an increase in NDCG@5 from 0.6818 to 0.7904 compared to GPR.

Ablation studies confirmed that the multi-task/target-aware generation (Step 2) and unified ranking (Step 3) components, especially the distributional consistency loss, were crucial for performance gains.

Online A/B tests on Tencent's Weixin Channels demonstrated a statistically significant +1.3427% increase in GMV-Normal at 5% traffic and +0.3500% at 80% traffic, leading to full production deployment.
solution: OneRanker proposes a three-step architecture for deep, architectural-level integration of generation and ranking.

A multi-task/target-aware enhancement module is introduced within the generation pipeline, using task tokens to decouple interest and value objectives and 'fake item tokens' for coarse-grained target awareness.

A unified ranking stage employs a dedicated ranking decoder for fine-grained business value alignment, coupled with a triadic joint loss function (MTP, BPR, Distributional Consistency) to optimize the end-to-end system.
paper_summary.summary: The OneRanker framework by Tencent Inc. integrates generation and ranking into a single end-to-end model for industrial advertising recommendation, addressing limitations of existing generative methods through value-aware multi-task learning and a unified ranking stage. It achieved a 44.7% relative improvement in offline HR@1 and a +1.3427% increase in GMV-Normal during online A/B testing on Tencent's Weixin Channels advertising system, leading to its full deployment.

title: OneRanker: Unified Generation and Ranking with One Model in Industrial Advertising Recommendation

abstract: Large-scale recommendations commonly adopt a multi-stage cascading ranking system paradigm to balance effectiveness and efficiency. Early Stage Ranking (ESR) systems utilize the "user-item decoupling" approach, where independently learned user and item representations are only combined at the final layer. While efficient, this design is limited in effectiveness, as it struggles to capture fine-grained user-item affinities and cross-signals. To address these, we propose the Generative Early Stage Ranking (GESR) paradigm, introducing the Mixture of Attention (MoA) module which leverages diverse attention mechanisms to bridge the effectiveness gap: the Hard Matching Attention (HMA) module encodes explicit cross-signals by computing raw match counts between user and item features; the Target-Aware Self Attention module generates target-aware user representations conditioned on the item, enabling more personalized learning; and the Cross Attention modules facilitate early and more enriched interactions between user-item features. MoA's specialized attention encodings are further refined in the final layer through a Multi-Logit Parameterized Gating (MLPG) module, which integrates the newly learned embeddings via gating and produces secondary logits that are fused with the primary logit. To address the efficiency and latency challenges, we have introduced a comprehensive suite of optimization techniques. These span from custom kernels that maximize the capabilities of the latest hardware to efficient serving solutions powered by caching mechanisms. The proposed GESR paradigm has shown substantial improvements in topline metrics, engagement, and consumption tasks, as validated by both offline and online experiments. To the best of our knowledge, this marks the first successful deployment of full target-aware attention sequence modeling within an ESR stage at such a scale.
title: Generative Early Stage Ranking

abstract: Generative Recommendation has emerged as a promising paradigm, reformulating recommendation as a sequence-to-sequence generation task over hierarchical Semantic IDs. However, existing methods suffer from a critical issue we term Semantic Drift, where errors in early, high-level tokens irreversibly divert the generation trajectory into irrelevant semantic subspaces. Inspired by Process Reward Models (PRMs) that enhance reasoning in Large Language Models, we propose Promise, a novel framework that integrates dense, step-by-step verification into generative models. Promise features a lightweight PRM to assess the quality of intermediate inference steps, coupled with a PRM-guided Beam Search strategy that leverages dense feedback to dynamically prune erroneous branches. Crucially, our approach unlocks Test-Time Scaling Laws for recommender systems: by increasing inference compute, smaller models can match or surpass larger models. Extensive offline experiments and online A/B tests on a large-scale platform demonstrate that Promise effectively mitigates Semantic Drift, significantly improving recommendation accuracy while enabling efficient deployment.

keyInsights: The concept of Process Reward Models, successful in LLMs for multi-step reasoning, can effectively mitigate semantic drift in generative recommendation by providing dense, step-by-step feedback during hierarchical SID generation.

Intelligent augmentation of inference-time computation through PRM-guided search unlocks 'Test-Time Scaling Laws' in generative recommendation, allowing smaller models to achieve comparable performance to larger ones more efficiently.

By evaluating and pruning an expanded set of candidates while keeping the core decoder's beam size small, significant quality improvements can be achieved during inference without a proportional increase in overall computational burden.
originalProblem: Generative Recommendation Systems (GRS) that use hierarchical Semantic IDs (SIDs) suffer from 'Semantic Drift', where early generation errors propagate.
This drift is caused by exposure bias, a discrepancy between training (ground-truth conditioning) and inference (self-generated token conditioning).
Existing generative models lack mechanisms to verify and correct intermediate steps in the autoregressive SID generation process.
results: PROMISE consistently outperforms baselines on public datasets, demonstrating improvements such as +44.39% NDCG@5 over ActionPiece on Sports and Outdoors.

On Kuaishou's industrial platform, the model achieves +40.47% NDCG@100 over strong baselines, and online A/B tests show statistically significant increases in core business metrics like +0.121% total app usage time.

Test-time scaling via PROMISE is more efficient than parameter scaling, achieving better performance for comparable FLOPs while adding only a marginal 15% to total parameters and 10% to inference latency.
solution: PROMISE introduces a lightweight, path-level Process Reward Model (PRM) trained jointly with the generative backbone to evaluate the quality of intermediate SID generation steps.
The PRM is optimized using an InfoNCE loss on both positive (ground-truth prefixes) and strategically sampled negative intermediate SID paths.
During inference, a PRM-guided beam search generates an expanded set of candidate SIDs and uses the PRM to prune them, selecting only high-quality paths to proceed to the next decoding step.
paper_summary.summary: PROMISE, developed by Kuaishou Inc., addresses semantic drift in generative recommendation systems by integrating process reward models and a PRM-guided beam search strategy. The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in offline benchmarks and improves user engagement by over 0.1% in online A/B tests, while demonstrating efficient test-time scaling for enhanced inference.

title: PROMISE: Process Reward Models Unlock Test-Time Scaling Laws in Generative Recommendations

abstract:
Generative recommendation is an emerging paradigm that leverages the
extensive knowledge of large language models by formulating recommendations
into a text-to-text generation task. However, existing studies face two key
limitations in (i) incorporating implicit item relationships and (ii) utilizing
rich yet lengthy item information. To address these challenges, we propose a
Generative Recommender via semantic-Aware Multi-granular late fusion (GRAM),
introducing two synergistic innovations. First, we design semantic-to-lexical
translation to encode implicit hierarchical and collaborative item
relationships into the vocabulary space of LLMs. Second, we present
multi-granular late fusion to integrate rich semantics efficiently with minimal
information loss. It employs separate encoders for multi-granular prompts,
delaying the fusion until the decoding stage. Experiments on four benchmark
datasets show that GRAM outperforms eight state-of-the-art generative
recommendation models, achieving significant improvements of 11.5-16.0% in
Recall@5 and 5.3-13.6% in NDCG@5. The source code is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Mapping recommendation semantics directly to LLM vocabulary space enables better utilization of pre-trained linguistic knowledge for generating semantically coherent recommendations
Delaying information fusion until the decoding stage dramatically reduces computational complexity while preserving comprehensive item information during generation
originalProblem: LLMs struggle with recommendation-specific semantics like hierarchical product taxonomies and collaborative filtering patterns when using numeric or out-of-vocabulary item IDs
Existing methods face computational challenges when processing lengthy item metadata due to quadratic complexity in Transformers, leading to information loss through truncation or summarization
results: Consistent outperformance across Amazon Beauty, Toys and Games, Sports and Outdoors, and Yelp datasets against 14 baseline methods including state-of-the-art generative recommenders
Hierarchical semantics alone boost NDCG@5 by up to 27.2%, while collaborative semantics add up to 10.8% additional improvement in ablation studies
solution: Semantic-to-lexical translation converts implicit hierarchical and collaborative item relationships into natural language tokens from the LLM's vocabulary using hierarchical k-means clustering and TF-IDF-based token selection

Multi-granular late fusion separately processes coarse-grained user prompts (sequential item IDs) and fine-grained item prompts (detailed metadata) before combining them at the decoder stage through cross-attention
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sungkyunkwan University and Samsung Research develop GRAM, a generative recommendation framework that enables LLMs to capture implicit item relationships and efficiently process rich metadata, achieving 11.5-16.0% gains in Recall@5 and 5.3-13.6% improvements in NDCG@5 across four benchmark datasets through semantic-to-lexical translation that converts hierarchical clustering and collaborative filtering signals into natural language tokens within the LLM's vocabulary space, combined with multi-granular late fusion that separately encodes coarse-grained user history and fine-grained item details before integrating them at the decoder stage, reducing online computational complexity by 81x compared to early fusion approaches while demonstrating particularly strong performance on tail item recommendations with up to 47.8% NDCG@5 improvements over baselines.

title: GRAM: Generative Recommendation via Semantic-aware Multi-granular Late  Fusion

abstract:
We introduce SessionRec, a novel next-session prediction paradigm (NSPP) for
generative sequential recommendation, addressing the fundamental misalignment
between conventional next-item prediction paradigm (NIPP) and real-world
recommendation scenarios. Unlike NIPP's item-level autoregressive generation
that contradicts actual session-based user interactions, our framework
introduces a session-aware representation learning through hierarchical
sequence aggregation (intra/inter-session), reducing attention computation
complexity while enabling implicit modeling of massive negative interactions,
and a session-based prediction objective that better captures users' diverse
interests through multi-item recommendation in next sessions. Moreover, we
found that incorporating a rank loss for items within the session under the
next session prediction paradigm can significantly improve the ranking
effectiveness of generative sequence recommendation models. We also verified
that SessionRec exhibits clear power-law scaling laws similar to those observed
in LLMs. Extensive experiments conducted on public datasets and online A/B test
in Meituan App demonstrate the effectiveness of SessionRec. The proposed
paradigm establishes new foundations for developing industrial-scale generative
recommendation systems through its model-agnostic architecture and
computational efficiency.

keyInsights: Shifting the prediction objective from a single "next item" to an entire "next session" (Next Session Prediction Paradigm) fundamentally aligns the model with real-world user behavior and yields superior recommendation performance.

Hierarchical sequence encoding (item-level to session-level) dramatically reduces computational complexity for Transformer-based models, making them efficient for long user histories at industrial scale.

Incorporating a novel rank loss alongside retrieval loss effectively utilizes implicit negative interactions, enabling a single generative model to optimize for both item retrieval and intra-session ranking.
originalProblem: Conventional sequential recommendation (Next Item Prediction Paradigm) predicts a single "next item," which mismatches real-world user behavior of interacting with multiple items simultaneously within a "session."
Transformer-based models for sequential recommendation incur high computational complexity (O(N^2)) with increasing sequence length, hindering industrial deployment with long user histories.
Existing models often underutilize rich information from implicit negative interactions (exposed but not clicked items), leading to sub-optimal ranking.
results: SessionRec-HSTU consistently outperformed strong baselines on public datasets, achieving up to 49.98% higher Recall@500 and 31.45% higher NDCG@500 compared to the best NIPP baselines.

Achieved significant computational efficiency by reducing training time, making it suitable for industrial-scale deployment, and demonstrated plug-and-play compatibility with various backbone architectures.

Successfully deployed in the Meituan App's homepage recommendation system, leading to a 0.603% increase in Page Views for Payment (Pay PV) and a 0.564% increase in Page View Click-Through Conversion Rate (PVCTCVR) in online A/B tests.
solution: Introduces the Next Session Prediction Paradigm (NSPP), where the model's goal is to predict the entire set of positive interactions within a user's next session.

Employs a hierarchical sequence aggregation mechanism (Item-based Session Encoder and Session-based Sequence Encoder) to reduce the effective input sequence length, improving computational efficiency for Transformer backbones.

Combines a sampled cross-entropy retrieval loss with a novel rank loss that distinguishes positive items from negatively interacted ones within the same session, enabling unified retrieval and ranking.
paper_summary.summary: SessionRec introduces a Next Session Prediction Paradigm (NSPP) for generative sequential recommendation, aiming to predict an entire session of user interactions rather than a single next item. It achieves superior performance, with up to 49.98% improvement in Recall@500 on KuaiSAR and 34.98% on RecFlow over baselines, while significantly reducing computational complexity and showing positive online A/B test results on the Meituan App.

title: SessionRec: Next Session Prediction Paradigm For Generative Sequential  Recommendation

abstract: Recent advancements in large language model-based recommendation systems often represent items as text or semantic IDs and generate recommendations in an auto-regressive manner. However, due to the left-to-right greedy decoding strategy and the unidirectional logical flow, such methods often fail to produce globally optimal recommendations. In contrast, human reasoning does not follow a rigid left-to-right sequence. Instead, it often begins with keywords or intuitive insights, which are then refined and expanded. Inspired by this fact, we propose MindRec, a diffusion-driven coarse-to-fine generative paradigm that emulates human thought processes. Built upon a diffusion language model, MindRec departs from auto-regressive generation by leveraging a masked diffusion process to reconstruct items in a flexible, non-sequential manner. Particularly, our method first generates key tokens that reflect user preferences, and then expands them into the complete item, enabling adaptive and human-like generation. To further emulate the structured nature of human decision-making, we organize items into a hierarchical category tree. This structure guides the model to first produce the coarse-grained category and then progressively refine its selection through finer-grained subcategories before generating the specific item. To mitigate the local optimum problem inherent in greedy decoding, we design a novel beam search algorithm, Diffusion Beam Search, tailored for our mind-inspired generation paradigm. Experimental results demonstrate that MindRec yields a 9.5\% average improvement in top-1 accuracy over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its potential to enhance recommendation performance. The implementation is available via this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Diffusion-driven, non-autoregressive generation empirically outperforms auto-regressive methods for recommendation by allowing flexible item reconstruction and overcoming the limitations of rigid, left-to-right decoding.

The integration of hierarchical category information during both training and a coarse-to-fine inference approach substantially enhances recommendation accuracy and diversity by providing structured contextual guidance.

The custom Diversity-Penalized Diffusion Beam Search effectively mitigates the local optimum problem inherent in generative decoding, promoting a broader and more diverse exploration of the candidate space.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based generative recommender systems primarily rely on auto-regressive generation, leading to locally optimal sequences and the premature exclusion of globally relevant items.
The rigid, unidirectional generation process of current LLM-Rec methods struggles to capture holistic user intent and diverges from the flexible, iterative nature of human decision-making.
Traditional greedy decoding strategies in generative recommendation often restrict the model's ability to explore diverse and globally optimal item sequences.
results: MindRec consistently demonstrated superior performance across three real-world Amazon datasets, achieving an average 9.5% improvement in top-1 accuracy (HR@1) over state-of-the-art LLM-based generative recommenders.

LLaDA-based implementations, including MindRec, significantly outperformed auto-regressive LLM counterparts, validating the paper's premise that diffusion-driven generation offers a more effective recommendation paradigm.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of structured preference learning with hierarchical categories and the efficacy of the Diffusion Beam Search, particularly its diversity-penalized variant, in enhancing both recommendation accuracy and diversity.
solution: MindRec introduces a diffusion-driven coarse-to-fine generative framework, inspired by human reasoning, which utilizes a Large Language Diffusion with masking (LLaDA) model for non-sequential item reconstruction.

Items are represented using hierarchical category paths and semantic IDs, enabling structured preference learning and a two-phase inference process that first generates coarse categories and then refines semantic IDs.

A novel Diversity-Penalized Diffusion Beam Search algorithm is proposed to improve decoding efficiency and quality by mitigating local optima and encouraging a broader, more diverse exploration of candidate sequences.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Science and Technology of China developed MindRec, a diffusion-driven, coarse-to-fine generative framework that enhances recommendation accuracy by emulating human reasoning and overcoming auto-regressive generation limitations. The system achieved a 9.5% average improvement in top-1 accuracy across various datasets compared to existing methods.

title: MindRec: A Diffusion-driven Coarse-to-Fine Paradigm for Generative Recommendation

abstract: The retrieval phase is a vital component in recommendation systems, requiring the model to be effective and efficient. Recently, generative retrieval has become an emerging paradigm for document retrieval, showing notable performance. These methods enjoy merits like being end-to-end differentiable, suggesting their viability in recommendation. However, these methods fall short in efficiency and effectiveness for large-scale recommendations. To obtain efficiency and effectiveness, this paper introduces a generative retrieval framework, namely SEATER, which learns SEmAntic Tree-structured item identifiERs via contrastive learning. Specifically, we employ an encoder-decoder model to extract user interests from historical behaviors and retrieve candidates via tree-structured item identifiers. SEATER devises a balanced k-ary tree structure of item identifiers, allocating semantic space to each token individually. This strategy maintains semantic consistency within the same level, while distinct levels correlate to varying semantic granularities. This structure also maintains consistent and fast inference speed for all items. Considering the tree structure, SEATER learns identifier tokens' semantics, hierarchical relationships, and inter-token dependencies. To achieve this, we incorporate two contrastive learning tasks with the generation task to optimize both the model and identifiers. The infoNCE loss aligns the token embeddings based on their hierarchical positions. The triplet loss ranks similar identifiers in desired orders. In this way, SEATER achieves both efficiency and effectiveness. Extensive experiments on three public datasets and an industrial dataset have demonstrated that SEATER outperforms state-of-the-art models significantly.

keyInsights: Explicitly learning and embedding the hierarchical semantics of item identifiers via a balanced tree structure and contrastive losses significantly enhances retrieval effectiveness.

A balanced k-ary tree for identifiers, combined with a single-layer Transformer architecture, enables state-of-the-art generative retrieval performance with superior efficiency compared to deeper, less structure-aware models.

Constructing identifier trees using item embeddings derived from collaborative filtering (e.g., SASREC) is more effective than random or textual embeddings, suggesting that user interaction patterns provide valuable structural information for identifier construction.
originalProblem: Existing generative retrieval models treat item identifiers as plain text, neglecting to integrate their intrinsic structural semantics into the training process, leading to a shallow understanding of item relationships.

Current generative methods often create imbalanced identifier tree structures, resulting in inconsistent identifier lengths and variable, potentially increased, item inference times, which is problematic for large-scale systems.

Many generative retrieval models rely on multiple Transformer layers, incurring substantial computational costs during inference that limit their applicability in highly efficient large-scale recommender systems.
results: SEATER achieved state-of-the-art performance across all tested datasets and metrics, demonstrating up to 25.50% relative improvement in R@50 on the large-scale industrial Micro-Video dataset.
The multi-task contrastive learning scheme, specifically the Alignment Loss and Ranking Loss, proved essential, with their removal leading to significant performance drops in ablation studies.

The balanced k-ary tree identifier structure and unique token embedding strategy were validated as critical components, outperforming adaptations using DSI/NCI identifier structures by over 10% on average, while using only a single Transformer layer.
solution: Developed SEATER (SEmAntic Tree-structured item identifiERs), a generative retrieval framework that constructs item identifiers based on a balanced k-ary tree using hierarchical clustering of item embeddings.

Introduced two novel contrastive losses: an Alignment Loss to teach hierarchical semantics between parent-child tokens, and a Ranking Loss to differentiate similar identifiers based on shared prefix lengths.
Utilized a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model with only one layer for both encoder and decoder to maintain high efficiency while leveraging behavioral sequences for user interest representation.
paper_summary.summary: A new generative retrieval framework, SEATER, learns semantic tree-structured item identifiers and employs contrastive learning to enhance efficiency and effectiveness in recommender systems. It achieves state-of-the-art retrieval performance with a single Transformer layer, yielding up to 25.50% relative improvement in R@50 on an industrial dataset compared to existing methods.

title: Generative Retrieval with Semantic Tree-Structured Item Identifiers via Contrastive Learning

abstract:
Search-based recommendation is one of the most critical application scenarios in e-commerce platforms. Users' complex search contexts--such as spatiotemporal factors, historical interactions, and current query's information--constitute an essential part of their decision-making, reflecting implicit preferences that complement explicit query terms. Modeling such rich contextual signals and their intricate associations with candidate items remains a key challenge. Although numerous efforts have been devoted to building more effective search methods, existing approaches still show limitations in integrating contextual information, which hinders their ability to fully capture user intent.
To address these challenges, we propose a context-aware reasoning-enhanced generative search framework for better \textbf{understanding the complicated context}. Specifically, the framework first unifies heterogeneous user and item contexts into textual representations or text-based semantic identifiers and aligns them. To overcome the lack of explicit reasoning trajectories, we introduce a self-evolving post-training paradigm that iteratively combines supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning to progressively enhance the model's reasoning capability. In addition, we identify potential biases in existing RL algorithms when applied to search scenarios and present a debiased variant of GRPO to improve ranking performance. Extensive experiments on search log data collected from a real-world e-commerce platform demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance compared with strong baselines, validating its effectiveness for search-based recommendation.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: Towards Context-aware Reasoning-enhanced Generative Searching in E-commerce

abstract: Generative Recommendation has revolutionized recommender systems by reformulating retrieval as a sequence generation task over discrete item identifiers. Despite the progress, existing approaches typically rely on static, decoupled tokenization that ignores collaborative signals. While recent methods attempt to integrate collaborative signals into item identifiers either during index construction or through end-to-end modeling, they encounter significant challenges in real-world production environments. Specifically, the volatility of collaborative signals leads to unstable tokenization, and current end-to-end strategies often devolve into suboptimal two-stage training rather than achieving true co-evolution. To bridge this gap, we propose PIT, a dynamic Personalized Item Tokenizer framework for end-to-end generative recommendation, which employs a co-generative architecture that harmonizes collaborative patterns through collaborative signal alignment and synchronizes item tokenizer with generative recommender via a co-evolution learning. This enables the dynamic, joint, end-to-end evolution of both index construction and recommendation. Furthermore, a one-to-many beam index ensures scalability and robustness, facilitating seamless integration into large-scale industrial deployments. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that PIT consistently outperforms competitive baselines. In a large-scale deployment at Kuaishou, an online A/B test yielded a substantial 0.402% uplift in App Stay Time, validating the framework's effectiveness in dynamic industrial environments.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Aligning collaborative signals directly into item embeddings through the CSA module is crucial for bridging the semantic gap and improving recommendation relevance.
User-guided minimum-loss selection effectively filters generative noise and optimizes the alignment between item identifiers and user preferences, enabling true co-evolution.
A dynamic, one-to-many beam index is fundamental for capturing complex item semantics and providing robustness against concept drifts in highly dynamic environments.
originalProblem: Existing generative recommender systems often rely on static item tokenization, creating a semantic gap where content-optimized identifiers do not align with user interaction patterns.
Current end-to-end tokenization methods struggle to adapt to rapid concept drifts and volatile collaborative signals in dynamic industrial environments, leading to index instability.
Many 'end-to-end' approaches devolve into suboptimal two-stage or alternating optimizations, preventing true, concurrent co-evolution between item tokenization and the generative recommender.
results: PIT consistently achieved the best performance across all datasets and metrics (Recall@K, NDCG@K) in offline experiments compared to various traditional and generative recommendation models.
Ablation studies confirmed the essential contributions of the Collaborative Signal Alignment, Minimum-Loss Selection, and Dynamic Beam Index components to the overall performance.

Online A/B tests at Kuaishou demonstrated substantial business impact, including a 0.402% uplift in App Stay Time and significant improvements in long-term watch time, video views, likes, follows, and comments.
solution: A co-generative architecture integrates a Collaborative Signal Alignment (CSA) module to inject user interaction patterns into item embeddings, an Item-to-Token model for index generation, and a User-to-Token model as the generative recommender.

A co-evolution learning strategy uses a warm-up phase followed by a dynamic generation-selection-optimization loop, where a Minimum-Loss Selection (MLS) mechanism guides the item tokenizer based on user preferences.

A Dynamic Beam Index maintains a one-to-many relationship between items and SIDs, using loss-based competitive weighting and graph integrity pruning to adapt to semantic changes and ensure index stability.
paper_summary.summary: PIT introduces a dynamic personalized item tokenizer that enables the co-evolution of item tokenization and generative recommendation, addressing the semantic gap and volatility in large-scale industrial recommender systems. The framework demonstrated superior offline performance and achieved a 0.402% uplift in App Stay Time during online A/B tests at Kuaishou, validating its real-world effectiveness.

title: PIT: A Dynamic Personalized Item Tokenizer for End-to-End Generative Recommendation

abstract:
Generative Recommendation (GR) has become a promising end-to-end approach with high FLOPS utilization for resource-efficient recommendation. Despite the effectiveness, we show that current GR models suffer from a critical \textbf{bias amplification} issue, where token-level bias escalates as token generation progresses, ultimately limiting the recommendation diversity and hurting the user experience. By comparing against the key factor behind the success of traditional multi-stage pipelines, we reveal two limitations in GR that can amplify the bias: homogeneous reliance on the encoded history, and fixed computational budgets that prevent deeper user preference understanding.
To combat the bias amplification issue, it is crucial for GR to 1) incorporate more heterogeneous information, and 2) allocate greater computational resources at each token generation step. To this end, we propose CARE, a simple yet effective cascaded reasoning framework for debiased GR. To incorporate heterogeneous information, we introduce a progressive history encoding mechanism, which progressively incorporates increasingly fine-grained history information as the generation process advances. To allocate more computations, we propose a query-anchored reasoning mechanism, which seeks to perform a deeper understanding of historical information through parallel reasoning steps. We instantiate CARE on three GR backbones. Empirical results on four datasets show the superiority of CARE in recommendation accuracy, diversity, efficiency, and promising scalability. The codes and datasets are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Generative recommender systems exhibit a critical 'bias amplification' problem at the token level, which escalates as item tokens are generated from coarse-grained to fine-grained semantics.

Diagnosing GR through the 'lens of cascaded ranking' reveals that a primary cause of bias amplification is the homogeneous reliance on a fixed user history and a uniform computational budget across all token generation steps.

Integrating sophisticated, yet efficient, reasoning capabilities directly within each token generation step (rather than as a pre-processing step) can effectively combat bias amplification and simultaneously enhance recommendation accuracy and diversity.
originalProblem: Generative Recommender (GR) models suffer from significant 'bias amplification' at the token level, disproportionately assigning high generation probabilities to popular tokens, which worsens from coarse-grained to fine-grained semantics and limits diversity.
Current GR models rely on homogeneous encoded user history and fixed computational budgets for all token generation steps, preventing a progressively deeper understanding of user preferences.

Existing reasoning-based recommendation methods are often computationally expensive (e.g., multiple autoregressive passes) or do not directly address the unique manifestation of bias in semantic-ID-based GR models.
