results: CARE consistently achieved superior recommendation accuracy (Recall@K and NDCG@K) and significantly improved diversity (DivR@K) while substantially reducing the over-recommendation ratio (ORR@K) across four real-world datasets when integrated with GR backbones.

Analysis of token distributions showed that CARE effectively alleviated token-level bias amplification, with generated distributions becoming closer to ground-truth test distributions, particularly for later, fine-grained tokens.

The framework maintained high efficiency, exhibiting only an average 4.9% increase in training time and 1.5% in inference time, with minimal GPU memory increases, validating its practical scalability for real-time recommendation systems.
solution: Introduced CARE (CAscaded REasoning framework) which integrates 'Query-anchored Reasoning' by using learnable query vectors within a single forward pass, efficiently enriching computations for deeper user preference understanding without prohibitive latency.

Employed 'Progressive History Encoding' by dynamically selecting and encoding increasingly fine-grained historical item tokens at each generation stage, providing heterogeneous information that mirrors cascaded ranking.

Utilized a combined training objective that includes a standard recommendation loss and a novel 'Reasoning Diversity Loss,' which penalizes the cosine similarity between query vectors to promote diverse reasoning and combat token bias.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Science and Technology of China, National University of Singapore, Renmin University of China, and Meta AI developed CARE, a cascaded reasoning framework for generative recommender systems. CARE effectively mitigates popularity bias amplification in semantic-ID-based generative models by integrating query-anchored reasoning and progressive history encoding, leading to improved recommendation diversity and accuracy while maintaining high efficiency.

title: Bringing Reasoning to Generative Recommendation Through the Lens of Cascaded Ranking

abstract: The dominant retrieve-then-rank pipeline in large-scale recommender systems suffers from mis-calibration and engineering overhead due to its architectural split and differing optimization objectives. While recent generative sequence models have shown promise in unifying retrieval and ranking by auto-regressively generating ranked items, existing solutions typically address either personalized search or query-free recommendation, often exhibiting performance trade-offs when attempting to unify both. We introduce \textit{SynerGen}, a novel generative recommender model that bridges this critical gap by providing a single generative backbone for both personalized search and recommendation, while simultaneously excelling at retrieval and ranking tasks. Trained on behavioral sequences, our decoder-only Transformer leverages joint optimization with InfoNCE for retrieval and a hybrid pointwise-pairwise loss for ranking, allowing semantic signals from search to improve recommendation and vice versa. We also propose a novel time-aware rotary positional embedding to effectively incorporate time information into the attention mechanism. \textit{SynerGen} achieves significant improvements on widely adopted recommendation and search benchmarks compared to strong generative recommender and joint search and recommendation baselines. This work demonstrates the viability of a single generative foundation model for industrial-scale unified information access.

keyInsights: A single generative Transformer backbone can effectively unify personalized search and recommendation, eliminating the need for separate retrieve-then-rank stages without sacrificing performance.
Fusing frozen pre-trained semantic embeddings with trainable collaborative ID embeddings is crucial for robust performance across both frequent and long-tail items and diverse tasks.
Explicitly modeling fine-grained temporal dynamics using time-aware RoPE significantly enhances the model's ability to capture evolving user intent and session context.
originalProblem: Traditional retrieve-then-rank systems for search and recommendation suffer from architectural splits, misaligned optimization objectives, and high engineering overhead.
Existing generative recommenders often silo personalized search and query-free recommendation, leading to performance trade-offs when unification is attempted.
Effectively incorporating fine-grained temporal information and robustly fusing semantic and collaborative signals without performance degradation remains a challenge in unified models.
results: SynerGen achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive Recall and NDCG on public benchmarks (e.g., Book Review, eBook Search Sessions) for both recommendation and search, outperforming larger generative models and specialized baselines.

Ablation studies on a large-scale real-world e-commerce dataset (Session-US) confirm the critical contributions of collaborative embeddings, time-aware RoPE, and the joint training of retrieval and ranking heads.

The model demonstrates industrial viability and scalability, being trained on 16 NVIDIA A100 GPUs and evaluated on a dataset with 155 million users and 252 million items, while meeting strict latency constraints.
solution: A decoder-only Transformer backbone integrates item, action, time, and query inputs using a hybrid embedding design that combines frozen LLaMA-based semantic embeddings with optimized collaborative embeddings.
Task-specific tokens (Context, Retrieval, Ranking) explicitly signal and support both query-free recommendation and query-aware search within a single autoregressive sequence.

Fine-grained temporal information is incorporated using time-aware Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) and a task-specific masking matrix, alongside a joint InfoNCE contrastive retrieval loss and a hybrid pointwise/pairwise ranking loss.
paper_summary.summary: SynerGen, developed by Amazon, introduces a single generative Transformer backbone that unifies personalized search and query-free recommendation, effectively fusing diverse signals and temporal dynamics. It achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance on public benchmarks and demonstrates industrial scalability on a large-scale e-commerce dataset.

title: SynerGen: Contextualized Generative Recommender for Unified Search and Recommendation

abstract: In large-scale industrial recommendation systems, retrieval must produce high-quality candidates from massive corpora under strict latency. Recently, Generative Retrieval (GR) has emerged as a viable alternative to Embedding-Based Retrieval (EBR), which quantizes items into a finite token space and decodes candidates autoregressively, providing a scalable path that explicitly models target-history interactions via cross-attention. However, deploying GR in short-video feeds remains challenged by long-short interest interference, context-induced noise in hierarchical SID generation, and the lack of explicit learning from exposed-but-unclicked feedback. To address these challenges, we propose DualGR, which combines (i) a Dual-Branch Long/Short-Term Router (DBR) with selective activation, (ii) Search-based SID Decoding (S2D) that constrains fine-level decoding within the current coarse bucket for efficiency and noise control, and (iii) an Exposure-aware Next-Token Prediction Loss (ENTP-Loss) that treats unclicked exposures as coarse-level hard negatives to promote timely interest fade-out. On the large-scale Kuaishou short-video recommendation system, DualGR has achieved outstanding performance. Online A/B testing shows +0.527% video views and +0.432% watch time lifts, validating DualGR as a practical and effective paradigm for industrial generative retrieval.
title: DualGR: Generative Retrieval with Long and Short-Term Interests Modeling

abstract: Most large-scale recommender systems follow a multi-stage cascade of retrieval, pre-ranking, ranking, and re-ranking. A key challenge at the pre-ranking stage arises from the heterogeneity of training instances sampled from coarse-grained retrieval results, fine-grained ranking signals, and exposure feedback. Our analysis reveals that prevailing pre-ranking methods, which indiscriminately mix heterogeneous samples, suffer from gradient conflicts: hard samples dominate training while easy ones remain underutilized, leading to suboptimal performance. We further show that the common practice of uniformly scaling model complexity across all samples is inefficient, as it overspends computation on easy cases and slows training without proportional gains. To address these limitations, this paper presents Heterogeneity-Aware Adaptive Pre-ranking (HAP), a unified framework that mitigates gradient conflicts through conflict-sensitive sampling coupled with tailored loss design, while adaptively allocating computational budgets across candidates. Specifically, HAP disentangles easy and hard samples, directing each subset along dedicated optimization paths. Building on this separation, it first applies lightweight models to all candidates for efficient coverage, and further engages stronger models on the hard ones, maintaining accuracy while reducing cost. This approach not only improves pre-ranking effectiveness but also provides a practical perspective on scaling strategies in industrial recommender systems. HAP has been deployed in the Toutiao production system for 9 months, yielding up to 0.4% improvement in user app usage duration and 0.05% in active days, without additional computational cost. We also release a large-scale industrial hybrid-sample dataset to enable the systematic study of source-driven candidate heterogeneity in pre-ranking.

keyInsights: The diverse difficulty of candidate items in pre-ranking data causes gradient conflicts that bias training towards hard negatives, leading to suboptimal model performance.
Allocating computational resources adaptively, using simpler models for easy candidates and complex models for hard ones, enhances both efficiency and effectiveness.
Explicitly harmonizing gradient contributions by separating easy and hard negative samples during contrastive learning significantly improves optimization stability and model generalization.
originalProblem: Pre-ranking stages in large-scale recommender systems face challenges from the inherent heterogeneity of candidates, comprising both easy and hard samples.
Indiscriminate mixing of these samples during training leads to 'gradient conflicts,' where hard negatives disproportionately dominate learning, causing suboptimal model optimization.
Blindly scaling model complexity across all candidates results in computational inefficiency, as high-capacity models are unnecessarily applied to easily distinguishable items.
results: HAP consistently surpassed state-of-the-art pre-ranking models across all metrics and test sets on the ToutiaoRec dataset, showing a 1.34% AUC improvement on hard negatives compared to the best baseline.
Online A/B tests in the Toutiao production system demonstrated practical gains, including a 3.0% increase in Click-Through Rate (CTR) and a 0.4% increase in app usage duration.
The framework achieved these effectiveness improvements while simultaneously reducing CPU usage by 6% and maintaining comparable serving latency for over nine months in real-world deployment.
The authors released the ToutiaoRec dataset, a large-scale, fully-annotated industrial dataset for multi-stage recommendation research.
solution: The Heterogeneity-Aware Adaptive Pre-ranking (HAP) framework introduces a unified approach with two core components: Gradient-Harmonized Contrastive Learning (GHCL) and Difficulty-Aware Model Routing (DAMR).
GHCL mitigates gradient conflicts by explicitly decoupling negative samples into hard and easy sets, computing independent contrastive losses for balanced optimization.

DAMR adaptively allocates computational resources via a two-stage model cascade: a lightweight model performs initial coarse filtering, passing only harder candidates to a more expressive model for fine-grained ranking.
paper_summary.summary: ByteDance researchers developed the Heterogeneity-Aware Adaptive Pre-ranking (HAP) framework, which tackles the diverse nature of candidates in recommender systems through specialized learning and adaptive model routing. Deployed in Toutiao, HAP improved click-through rates by 3.0% and reduced CPU usage by 6%, while also contributing the ToutiaoRec industrial dataset.

title: Not All Candidates are Created Equal: A Heterogeneity-Aware Approach to Pre-ranking in Recommender Systems

abstract: Semantic IDs serve as a key component in generative recommendation systems. They not only incorporate open-world knowledge from large language models (LLMs) but also compress the semantic space to reduce generation difficulty. However, existing methods suffer from two major limitations: (1) the lack of contextual awareness in generation tasks leads to a gap between the Semantic ID codebook space and the generation space, resulting in suboptimal recommendations; and (2) suboptimal quantization methods exacerbate semantic loss in LLMs. To address these issues, we propose Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs (DOS) method. Specifically, DOS employs a user-item dual flow-framework that leverages collaborative signals to align the Semantic ID codebook space with the generation space. Furthermore, we introduce an orthogonal residual quantization scheme that rotates the semantic space to an appropriate orientation, thereby maximizing semantic preservation. Extensive offline experiments and online A/B testing demonstrate the effectiveness of DOS. The proposed method has been successfully deployed in Meituan's mobile application, serving hundreds of millions of users.

keyInsights: Directly aligning SID learning with the downstream generative recommendation objective, rather than general reconstruction, and focusing on distilling task-relevant features significantly enhances performance.

The Orthogonal Residual Quantization (ORQ) module's approach of rotating semantic space and iteratively extracting features effectively disentangles and preserves critical semantic information during discrete token generation.

Integrating user behavior and target item information into a shared semantic space via a dual-flow architecture is crucial for generating context-aware and meaningful SIDs for personalized recommendations.

Surprisingly, adding a decoder for general reconstruction can degrade performance in task-specific SID learning, indicating that a more targeted quantization without a decoder is more effective for selecting task-relevant information.
originalProblem: Existing Semantic ID (SID) generation methods often learn SIDs in a task-agnostic manner, creating a 'Codebook-Generation Gap' that misaligns with the generative task.
The quantization process of continuous semantic representations into discrete SIDs leads to semantic information loss, as current techniques are not optimized for LLM comprehension in recommendation.
Current SID learning approaches inadequately leverage user-item collaborative signals, which are crucial for generating context-aware and personalized recommendations.
results: A one-week online A/B test on 30% of Meituan's production traffic showed a 1.15% increase in online revenue after deploying the DOS framework.
Offline experiments demonstrated superior performance on downstream recommendation tasks, achieving an AUC of 0.8763 and an F1-Score of 0.8057, outperforming baselines like DAS and RQ-VAE.
In generative settings, SIDs produced by DOS (HSTU-DOS) yielded a Hit@10 of 0.0676, indicating higher quality for next-token prediction compared to other methods like HSTU-DAS (0.0511).
solution: A Dual-Flow Integration (DFI) module was introduced to combine collaborative information from user click sequences and target items, aligning their representations in a unified semantic space using a shared codebook.

Orthogonal Residual Quantization (ORQ) was developed, which is a layer-wise module that rotates input features to an optimal orientation, selects primary task-relevant dimensions, and iteratively quantizes them while passing residuals and secondary features.

The model is trained using a joint loss function that includes Binary Cross-Entropy for the downstream prediction task, regularization for feature orthogonality and mutual information, and vector quantization loss, omitting a reconstruction decoder.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan researchers developed Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs (DOS), a framework that learns context-aware and semantically rich compact representations for generative recommendation systems. The method yielded a 1.15% increase in online revenue during an A/B test on Meituan's platform and achieved superior offline performance with an AUC of 0.8763 and an F1-Score of 0.8057.

title: DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan

abstract: Route recommendation systems commonly adopt a multi-stage pipeline involving fine-ranking and re-ranking to produce high-quality ordered recommendations. However, this paradigm faces three critical limitations. First, there is a misalignment between offline training objectives and online metrics. Offline gains do not necessarily translate to online improvements. Actual performance must be validated through A/B testing, which may potentially compromise the user experience. Second, redundancy elimination relies on rigid, handcrafted rules that lack adaptability to the high variance in user intent and the unstructured complexity of real-world scenarios. Third, the strict separation between fine-ranking and re-ranking stages leads to sub-optimal performance. Since each module is optimized in isolation, the fine-ranking stage remains oblivious to the list-level objectives (e.g., diversity) targeted by the re-ranker, thereby preventing the system from achieving a jointly optimized global optimum. To overcome these intertwined challenges, we propose SCASRec (Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Recommendation), a unified generative framework that integrates ranking and redundancy elimination into a single end-to-end process. SCASRec introduces a stepwise corrective reward (SCR) to guide list-wise refinement by focusing on hard samples, and employs a learnable End-of-Recommendation (EOR) token to terminate generation adaptively when no further improvement is expected. Experiments on two large-scale, open-sourced route recommendation datasets demonstrate that SCASRec establishes an SOTA in offline and online settings. SCASRec has been fully deployed in a real-world navigation app, demonstrating its effectiveness.
title: SCASRec: A Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Model for Generative Route List Recommendation

abstract:
Reranking plays a crucial role in modern multi-stage recommender systems by
rearranging the initial ranking list. Due to the inherent challenges of
combinatorial search spaces, some current research adopts an
evaluator-generator paradigm, with a generator generating feasible sequences
and an evaluator selecting the best sequence based on the estimated list
utility. However, these methods still face two issues. Firstly, due to the goal
inconsistency problem between the evaluator and generator, the generator tends
to fit the local optimal solution of exposure distribution rather than
combinatorial space optimization. Secondly, the strategy of generating target
items one by one is difficult to achieve optimality because it ignores the
information of subsequent items.
To address these issues, we propose a utilizing Neighbor Lists model for
Generative Reranking (NLGR), which aims to improve the performance of the
generator in the combinatorial space. NLGR follows the evaluator-generator
paradigm and improves the generator's training and generating methods.
Specifically, we use neighbor lists in combination space to enhance the
training process, making the generator perceive the relative scores and find
the optimization direction. Furthermore, we propose a novel sampling-based
non-autoregressive generation method, which allows the generator to jump
flexibly from the current list to any neighbor list. Extensive experiments on
public and industrial datasets validate NLGR's effectiveness and we have
successfully deployed NLGR on the Meituan food delivery platform.

keyInsights: Training a generator with relative reward signals derived from 'neighbor lists' effectively addresses the goal inconsistency problem, providing fine-grained optimization direction.
Non-autoregressive, sampling-based generation allows for more flexible exploration of the combinatorial space, overcoming the 'partial sight' limitation of sequential methods.
Confining the computationally intensive evaluator to offline training only enables efficient and low-latency online inference for the generator, critical for industrial deployment.
originalProblem: Existing generative reranking models suffer from 'goal inconsistency' where the evaluator supervises a generator aiming to transform a list, leading to suboptimal optimization.
Many generative models, especially autoregressive ones, only consider preceding items, leading to 'partial sight' and hindering global optimality in combinatorial list generation.
The exponential search space of item permutations makes brute-force optimization computationally prohibitive for large-scale industrial recommendation systems.
results: NLGR consistently outperformed all baseline models (e.g., DCDR) in offline experiments on both Meituan and Taobao Ad datasets, achieving higher AUC, NDCG, and Hit Ratio (e.g., HR@10% of 0.8369 on Meituan).
Online A/B tests on the Meituan food delivery platform demonstrated a +3.25% increase in Click-Through Rate (CTR) and a +3.07% increase in Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV).
The system maintained low latency (1.6 ms cost) and 0.0% time-out rate during online deployment, confirming its efficiency for real-time industrial use.
solution: Introduces NLGR-E (Evaluator) and NLGR-G (Generator) within an evaluator-generator paradigm, where NLGR-E is exclusively used for offline training of NLGR-G.

Proposes a sampling-based non-autoregressive generation process in NLGR-G, utilizing Position Decision Unit (PDU) to choose items for replacement and Candidate Retrieval Unit (CRU) to select new items.
Develops a novel 'neighbor lists' training method for NLGR-G, using relative reward signals (utility differences) from NLGR-E to guide the generator in optimizing list utility more effectively.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan's NLGR presents a generative reranking framework that employs a novel neighbor list training method to guide a non-autoregressive generator with relative reward signals. This approach resulted in a +3.25% CTR and +3.07% GMV increase during online A/B tests on the Meituan food delivery platform while maintaining real-time efficiency.

title: NLGR: Utilizing Neighbor Lists for Generative Rerank in Personalized  Recommendation Systems

abstract: Generative recommendation (GR) typically first quantizes continuous item embeddings into multi-level semantic IDs (SIDs), and then generates the next item via autoregressive decoding. Although existing methods are already competitive in terms of recommendation performance, directly inheriting the autoregressive decoding paradigm from language models still suffers from three key limitations: (1) autoregressive decoding struggles to jointly capture global dependencies among the multi-dimensional features associated with different positions of SID; (2) using a unified, fixed decoding path for the same item implicitly assumes that all users attend to item attributes in the same order; (3) autoregressive decoding is inefficient at inference time and struggles to meet real-time requirements. To tackle these challenges, we propose MDGR, a Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation framework that reshapes the GR pipeline from three perspectives: codebook, training, and inference. (1) We adopt a parallel codebook to provide a structural foundation for diffusion-based GR. (2) During training, we adaptively construct masking supervision signals along both the temporal and sample dimensions. (3) During inference, we develop a warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding strategy for efficient generation of SIDs. Extensive experiments on multiple public and industrial-scale datasets show that MDGR outperforms ten state-of-the-art baselines by up to 10.78%. Furthermore, by deploying MDGR on a large-scale online advertising platform, we achieve a 1.20% increase in revenue, demonstrating its practical value.
title: Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation

abstract: For multi-stage recommenders in industry, a user request would first trigger a simple and efficient retriever module that selects and ranks a list of relevant items, then the recommender calls a slower but more sophisticated reranking model that refines the item list exposure to the user. To consistently optimize the two-stage retrieval reranking framework, most efforts have focused on learning reranker-aware retrievers. In contrast, there has been limited work on how to achieve a retriever-aware reranker. In this work, we provide evidence that the retriever scores from the previous stage are informative signals that have been underexplored. Specifically, we first empirically show that the reranking task under the two-stage framework is naturally a noise reduction problem on the retriever scores, and theoretically show the limitations of naive utilization techniques of the retriever scores. Following this notion, we derive an adversarial framework DNR that associates the denoising reranker with a carefully designed noise generation module. The resulting DNR solution extends the conventional score error minimization loss with three augmented objectives, including: 1) a denoising objective that aims to denoise the noisy retriever scores to align with the user feedback; 2) an adversarial retriever score generation objective that improves the exploration in the retriever score space; and 3) a distribution regularization term that aims to align the distribution of generated noisy retriever scores with the real ones. We conduct extensive experiments on three public datasets and an industrial recommender system, together with analytical support, to validate the effectiveness of the proposed DNR.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: Denoising Neural Reranker for Recommender Systems

abstract: Sequential recommendation aims to predict a user's next action in large-scale recommender systems. While traditional methods often suffer from insufficient information interaction, recent generative recommendation models partially address this issue by directly generating item predictions. To better capture user intents, recent studies have introduced a reasoning process into generative recommendation, significantly improving recommendation performance. However, these approaches are constrained by the singularity of item semantic representations, facing challenges such as limited diversity in reasoning pathways and insufficient reliability in the reasoning process. To tackle these issues, we introduce REG4Rec, a reasoning-enhanced generative model that constructs multiple dynamic semantic reasoning paths alongside a self-reflection process, ensuring high-confidence recommendations. Specifically, REG4Rec utilizes an MoE-based parallel quantization codebook (MPQ) to generate multiple unordered semantic tokens for each item, thereby constructing a larger-scale diverse reasoning space. Furthermore, to enhance the reliability of reasoning, we propose a training reasoning enhancement stage, which includes Preference Alignment for Reasoning (PARS) and a Multi-Step Reward Augmentation (MSRA) strategy. PARS uses reward functions tailored for recommendation to enhance reasoning and reflection, while MSRA introduces future multi-step actions to improve overall generalization. During inference, Consistency-Oriented Self-Reflection for Pruning (CORP) is proposed to discard inconsistent reasoning paths, preventing the propagation of erroneous reasoning. Lastly, we develop an efficient offline training strategy for large-scale recommendation. Experiments on real-world datasets and online evaluations show that REG4Rec delivers outstanding performance and substantial practical value.

keyInsights: Enabling flexible, unordered item token representations through parallel expert models significantly enhances the capture of diverse item attributes and varying user intents in recommendation.

Reinforcement learning, guided by a multi-faceted reward system that considers semantic alignment, path robustness, and step-wise consistency across multiple future interactions, is effective for learning reliable reasoning processes.

Dynamically adapting numerical precision at a layer-wise level during training provides a superior balance between training acceleration and accuracy preservation compared to uniform quantization in large-scale industrial deployments.
originalProblem: Existing generative recommendation models use fixed semantic token sequences, leading to rigid item representations that struggle to capture diverse user intents and multifaceted item attributes.

Prior reasoning-enhanced generative recommendation methods often lack mechanisms to assess the reliability or consistency of their generated reasoning paths, potentially introducing noise and reducing accuracy.
Deploying complex deep learning recommendation models in large-scale industrial settings faces significant challenges in terms of scalability, training efficiency, and resource cost.
results: REG4Rec outperformed six state-of-the-art baselines, achieving average relative improvements of 10.06% in Recall@5 and 11.01% in Recall@10 across various public and industrial datasets.

The Layer-Adaptive Dynamic Quantization Controller (LADQ) achieved a 32.3% speedup in queries per second (QPS) over the FP32 baseline on the industrial dataset, with only a 1.3% relative drop in Recall@5, significantly outperforming uniform quantization.

Online A/B tests on Alibaba's advertising platform demonstrated statistically significant improvements (p < 0.05): +5.60% in Advertising Revenue, +1.81% in Click-Through Rate, and +3.29% in Gross Merchandise Volume.
solution: A Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) based Parallel Quantization Codebooks (MPQ) module generates diverse, unordered semantic tokens for items, complemented by Confidence-based Reasoning Step Selection (CRSS) for dynamic token ordering.

A two-stage training framework includes pre-training with auxiliary category prediction and post-training via Reinforcement Learning (RL) with Preference Alignment for Reasoning (PARS) and Multi-Step Reward Augmentation (MSRA) to ensure reliable and consistent reasoning paths.

A Layer-Adaptive Dynamic Quantization Controller (LADQ) is implemented to dynamically allocate numerical precision (fp32, bf16, fp8) layer-wise, accelerating training while preserving accuracy, and Consistency-Oriented Self-Reflection for Pruning (CORP) enhances inference reliability.
paper_summary.summary: REG4Rec, a model developed by Alibaba Group, introduces a reasoning-enhanced generative framework for large-scale recommendation systems capable of creating flexible and reliable item recommendation paths. It achieved up to 11.01% average relative improvement in Recall@10 over baselines in offline tests and significantly increased advertising revenue by 5.60% in online A/B tests on Alibaba's platform.

title: REG4Rec: Reasoning-Enhanced Generative Model for Large-Scale Recommendation Systems

abstract: Sequential recommender systems rank relevant items by modeling a user's interaction history and computing the inner product between the resulting user representation and stored item embeddings. To avoid the significant memory overhead of storing large item sets, the generative recommendation paradigm instead models each item as a series of discrete semantic codes. Here, the next item is predicted by an autoregressive model that generates the code sequence corresponding to the predicted item. However, despite promising ranking capabilities on small datasets, these methods have yet to surpass traditional sequential recommenders on large item sets, limiting their adoption in the very scenarios they were designed to address. To resolve this, we propose MSCGRec, a Multimodal Semantic and Collaborative Generative Recommender. MSCGRec incorporates multiple semantic modalities and introduces a novel self-supervised quantization learning approach for images based on the DINO framework. Additionally, MSCGRec fuses collaborative and semantic signals by extracting collaborative features from sequential recommenders and treating them as a separate modality. Finally, we propose constrained sequence learning that restricts the large output space during training to the set of permissible tokens. We empirically demonstrate on three large real-world datasets that MSCGRec outperforms both sequential and generative recommendation baselines and provide an extensive ablation study to validate the impact of each component.

keyInsights: Fusing collaborative signals directly as a modality within a generative framework is highly effective in closing the performance gap with traditional sequential recommenders.
Incorporating quantization directly into a self-supervised learning process (RQ-DINO) yields more semantically meaningful codes for modalities like images than post-hoc quantization.
Optimizing generative model training by constraining the softmax calculation to only valid code sequences significantly enhances efficiency and predictive performance for large item catalogs.
originalProblem: Traditional sequential recommenders face high memory and computational overhead with large item embedding tables, often underutilizing rich multimodal semantic information.

Existing generative recommenders, despite theoretical advantages, historically underperform traditional sequential models on large datasets due to unimodal focus and insufficient fusion of collaborative and semantic signals.
Semantic code quality, particularly for images, and training efficiency in sequence modeling for vast item sets remain challenges for the generative recommendation paradigm.
results: MSCGRec established state-of-the-art performance on large-scale datasets (Amazon 2023, PixelRec), consistently outperforming existing generative recommendation baselines across all metrics.
The framework is the first generative method shown to match or surpass traditional sequential recommenders like SASRec and BERT4Rec on large, real-world datasets.

Ablation studies confirmed that collaborative information is the strongest performance contributor, and both RQ-DINO image quantization and constrained sequence learning significantly enhance results.
solution: MSCGRec represents each item as unique discrete codes derived from multiple modalities (text, images, and collaborative signals) using Residual Quantization.
A novel self-supervised quantization learning approach, RQ-DINO, integrates Residual Quantization directly into the DINO framework for image encoding to generate semantically relevant codes.

Sequence modeling is improved by using a constrained training loss that restricts softmax normalization to permissible codes and by employing adapted positional embeddings for inter- and intra-item code relationships.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ETH Zurich and Meta AI developed MSCGRec, a multimodal generative recommender system that effectively fuses diverse semantic and collaborative signals. It is the first generative method to match or surpass traditional sequential recommenders on large, real-world datasets, demonstrating superior performance, such as an 11.1% Recall@1 improvement on the "Beauty" dataset compared to other generative approaches.

title: Multimodal Generative Recommendation for Fusing Semantic and Collaborative Signals

abstract: Generative Recommendation (GR) has emerged as a transformative paradigm that reformulates the traditional cascade ranking system into a sequence-to-item generation task, facilitated by the use of discrete Semantic IDs (SIDs). However, current SIDs are suboptimal as the indexing objectives (Stage 1) are misaligned with the actual recommendation goals (Stage 2). Since these identifiers remain static (Stage 2), the backbone model lacks the flexibility to adapt them to the evolving complexities of user interactions. Furthermore, the prevailing strategy of flattening hierarchical SIDs into token sequences leads to sequence length inflation, resulting in prohibitive computational overhead and inference latency. To address these challenges, we propose IntRR, a novel framework that integrates objective-aligned SID Redistribution and structural Length Reduction. By leveraging item-specific Unique IDs (UIDs) as collaborative anchors, this approach dynamically redistributes semantic weights across hierarchical codebook layers. Concurrently, IntRR handles the SID hierarchy recursively, eliminating the need to flatten sequences. This ensures a fixed cost of one token per item. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that IntRR yields substantial improvements over representative generative baselines, achieving superior performance in both recommendation accuracy and efficiency.
title: IntRR: A Framework for Integrating SID Redistribution and Length Reduction

abstract: Reranking plays a crucial role in modern recommender systems by capturing the mutual influences within the list. Due to the inherent challenges of combinatorial search spaces, most methods adopt a two-stage search paradigm: a simple General Search Unit (GSU) efficiently reduces the candidate space, and an Exact Search Unit (ESU) effectively selects the optimal sequence. These methods essentially involve making trade-offs between effectiveness and efficiency, while suffering from a severe \textbf{inconsistency problem}, that is, the GSU often misses high-value lists from ESU. To address this problem, we propose YOLOR, a one-stage reranking method that removes the GSU while retaining only the ESU. Specifically, YOLOR includes: (1) a Tree-based Context Extraction Module (TCEM) that hierarchically aggregates multi-scale contextual features to achieve "list-level effectiveness", and (2) a Context Cache Module (CCM) that enables efficient feature reuse across candidate permutations to achieve "permutation-level efficiency". Extensive experiments across public and industry datasets validate YOLOR's performance, and we have successfully deployed YOLOR on the Meituan food delivery platform.
title: You Only Evaluate Once: A Tree-based Rerank Method at Meituan

abstract: Recommendation systems are essential tools in modern e-commerce, facilitating personalized user experiences by suggesting relevant products. Recent advancements in generative models have demonstrated potential in enhancing recommendation systems; however, these models often exhibit limitations in optimizing retrieval tasks, primarily due to their reliance on autoregressive generation mechanisms. Conventional approaches introduce sequential dependencies that impede efficient retrieval, as they are inherently unsuitable for generating multiple items without positional constraints within a single request session. To address these limitations, we propose TBGRecall, a framework integrating Next Session Prediction (NSP), designed to enhance generative retrieval models for e-commerce applications. Our framework reformulation involves partitioning input samples into multi-session sequences, where each sequence comprises a session token followed by a set of item tokens, and then further incorporate multiple optimizations tailored to the generative task in retrieval scenarios. In terms of training methodology, our pipeline integrates limited historical data pre-training with stochastic partial incremental training, significantly improving training efficiency and emphasizing the superiority of data recency over sheer data volume. Our extensive experiments, conducted on public benchmarks alongside a large-scale industrial dataset from TaoBao, show TBGRecall outperforms the state-of-the-art recommendation methods, and exhibits a clear scaling law trend. Ultimately, NSP represents a significant advancement in the effectiveness of generative recommendation systems for e-commerce applications.

keyInsights: Re-framing generative retrieval from token-wise to session-wise prediction (NSP) effectively resolves the mismatch with real-world unordered item retrieval.
Task-specific architectural components like MSP and MoE are crucial for maximizing the performance of generative models in complex e-commerce recommendation scenarios.
Data recency, facilitated by efficient incremental training strategies like PIT, yields better model performance and faster deployment than relying solely on large historical data volumes.
originalProblem: Existing autoregressive generative models impose inappropriate sequential dependencies among items within a single retrieval session.
Efficiently updating massive recommendation models with continuously fresh data in large-scale industrial environments poses significant challenges.
The inherent mismatch between generative model's token-wise prediction and the unordered nature of retrieved item sets leads to suboptimal retrieval accuracy and efficiency.
results: TBGRecall outperformed state-of-the-art baselines on both the public RecFlow dataset and a massive Taobao industrial dataset, achieving HR@4000 of 29.45% on Taobao.
Partial Incremental Training (PIT) achieved competitive performance with full daily training using only 1/10th of the GPU resources and significantly improved performance over delayed training.
Online A/B tests on Taobao's 'Guess You Like' homepage demonstrated a +0.60% increase in Transaction Count and a +2.16% increase in Transaction Amount over a 7-day period.
solution: TBGRecall introduces Next Session Prediction (NSP), predicting the context for the next user session to remove intra-session item dependencies and align with retrieval.

The model incorporates architectural enhancements like Session Mask, session-wise Rotary Position Embedding (sw-RoPE), Token-Specific Network (TSN), Multi-Session Prediction (MSP), and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE).
Partial Incremental Training (PIT) is developed for efficient, continuous model updates using recent data, dramatically reducing training time and GPU resources.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiaotong University and Alibaba Inc. introduce TBGRecall, a generative retrieval model for e-commerce that aligns generative modeling with retrieval tasks via a Next Session Prediction (NSP) paradigm. The model achieved a +2.16% increase in transaction amount during online A/B tests on Taobao's homepage, demonstrating its efficacy in large-scale industrial settings.

title: TBGRecall: A Generative Retrieval Model for E-commerce Recommendation Scenarios

abstract: Generative retrieval (GR) has emerged as a promising paradigm in recommendation systems by autoregressively decoding identifiers of target items. Despite its potential, current approaches typically rely on the next-token prediction schema, which treats each token of the next interacted items as the sole target. This narrow focus 1) limits their ability to capture the nuanced structure of user preferences, and 2) overlooks the deep interaction between decoded identifiers and user behavior sequences. In response to these challenges, we propose RankGR, a Rank-enhanced Generative Retrieval method that incorporates listwise direct preference optimization for recommendation. RankGR decomposes the retrieval process into two complementary stages: the Initial Assessment Phase (IAP) and the Refined Scoring Phase (RSP). In IAP, we incorporate a novel listwise direct preference optimization strategy into GR, thus facilitating a more comprehensive understanding of the hierarchical user preferences and more effective partial-order modeling. The RSP then refines the top-{\lambda} candidates generated by IAP with interactions towards input sequences using a lightweight scoring module, leading to more precise candidate evaluation. Both phases are jointly optimized under a unified GR model, ensuring consistency and efficiency. Additionally, we implement several practical improvements in training and deployment, ultimately achieving a real-time system capable of handling nearly ten thousand requests per second. Extensive offline performance on both research and industrial datasets, as well as the online gains on the "Guess You Like" section of Taobao, validate the effectiveness and scalability of RankGR.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling hierarchical user preferences through Listwise Direct Preference Optimization (LDPO) is crucial, leading to an 18.28% improvement in Click HR@20 compared to not using it.
The Refined Scoring Phase (RSP) significantly enhances precision for top-K recommendations by enabling deep, candidate-centric interactions, evidenced by a 10.44% drop in Click HR@20 without it.
An optimal balance between the traditional next-token prediction and the novel preference alignment objective is essential for maximizing the model's performance.
originalProblem: Current generative retrieval (GR) models, using next-token prediction, inadequately capture hierarchical user preferences (e.g., purchase > click > expose).
These models exhibit shallow interactions between candidate items and user behavior sequences during inference, limiting personalized recommendations.
The prevalent GR architectures fail to effectively distinguish between varying degrees of item relevance for users.
results: RankGR achieved state-of-the-art offline performance, improving Click HR@20 by 28.7% over the best baseline (FORGE) on the large-scale Taobao dataset.
In an online A/B test on Taobao's "Guess You Like" section, RankGR increased Item Page Views by 1.08% and Transaction Count by 0.57%.
Ablation studies confirmed that both the Initial Assessment Phase (IAP) and Refined Scoring Phase (RSP) are crucial, with their removal leading to significant performance degradation.
solution: Implemented an Initial Assessment Phase (IAP) incorporating Listwise Direct Preference Optimization (LDPO) to directly model hierarchical user preferences across multiple feedback levels.
Designed a Refined Scoring Phase (RSP) with a deep interaction module that allows fine-grained, candidate-centric interactions with the complete user behavior sequence.
Deployed a unified generative model with an asynchronous pre-computation architecture and streaming training to ensure scalability and real-time performance in industrial settings.
paper_summary.summary: A collaborative effort by Zhejiang University and Alibaba Group, RankGR enhances generative retrieval by integrating listwise direct preference optimization for hierarchical user preferences and a refined scoring phase for deep candidate interaction. The system achieved state-of-the-art offline performance and, in online A/B tests on Taobao, increased Item Page Views by 1.08% and Transaction Count by 0.57%.

title: RankGR: Rank-Enhanced Generative Retrieval with Listwise Direct Preference Optimization in Recommendation

abstract: Recommendation is crucial for both user experience and company revenue in Meituan as a leading lifestyle company, and generative recommendation models (GRMs) are shown to produce quality recommendations recently. However, existing systems are limited by insufficient functionality support and inefficient implementations for training GRMs in industrial scenarios. As such, we introduce MTGenRec as an efficient and scalable system for GRM training. Specifically, to handle real-time insertions/deletions of sparse embeddings, MTGenRec employs dynamic hash tables to replace static ones. To improve training efficiency, MTGenRec conducts dynamic sequence balancing to address the computation load imbalances among GPUs and adopts feature ID deduplication alongside automatic table merging to accelerate embedding lookup. Extensive experiments show that MTGenRec improves training throughput by $1.6\times -- 2.4\times$ while achieving good scalability when running over 100 GPUs. MTGenRec has been deployed for many applications in Meituan and is now handling hundreds of millions of requests on a daily basis. On the delivery platform, we observe a 1.22% growth in user order volume and a 1.31% enhancement in online PV_CTR.
title: MTGenRec: An Efficient Distributed Training System for Generative Recommendation Models in Meituan

abstract: Re-ranking is critical in recommender systems for optimizing the order of recommendation lists, thus improving user satisfaction and platform revenue. Most existing methods follow a generator-evaluator paradigm, where the evaluator estimates the overall value of each candidate list. However, they often ignore the fact that users may exit before consuming the full list, leading to a mismatch between estimated generation value and actual consumption value. To bridge this gap, we propose CAVE, a personalized Consumption-Aware list Value Estimation framework. CAVE formulates the list value as the expectation over sub-list values, weighted by user-specific exit probabilities at each position. The exit probability is decomposed into an interest-driven component and a stochastic component, the latter modeled via a Weibull distribution to capture random external factors such as fatigue. By jointly modeling sub-list values and user exit behavior, CAVE yields a more faithful estimate of actual list consumption value. We further contribute three large-scale real-world list-wise benchmarks from the Kuaishou platform, varying in size and user activity patterns. Extensive experiments on these benchmarks, two Amazon datasets, and online A/B testing on Kuaishou show that CAVE consistently outperforms strong baselines, highlighting the benefit of explicitly modeling user exits in re-ranking.
title: From Generation to Consumption: Personalized List Value Estimation for Re-ranking

abstract: Generative recommendations (GR), which usually include item tokenizers and generative Large Language Models (LLMs), have demonstrated remarkable success across a wide range of scenarios. The majority of existing research efforts primarily concentrate on developing powerful item tokenizers or advancing LLM decoding strategies to attain superior performance. However, the critical fine-tuning step in GR frameworks, which is essential for adapting LLMs to recommendation data, remains largely unexplored. Current approaches predominantly rely on either the next-token prediction loss of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or recommendationspecific direct preference optimization (DPO) strategies. Both methods ignore the exploration of possible positive unobserved samples, which is commonly referred to as the exposure bias problem. To mitigate this problem, this paper treats the GR as a multi-step generation task and constructs a GFlowNets-based fine-tuning framework (GFlowGR). The proposed framework integrates collaborative knowledge from traditional recommender systems to create an adaptive trajectory sampler and a comprehensive reward model. Leveraging the diverse generation property of GFlowNets, along with sampling and heuristic weighting techniques, GFlowGR emerges as a promising approach to mitigate the exposure bias problem. Extensive empirical results on two real-world datasets and with two different GR backbones highlight the effectiveness and robustness of GFlowGR.

keyInsights: Generative Flow Networks provide a principled way to fine-tune LLMs for generative recommendation by learning from diverse, value-aware item sets and offering token-level supervision.
A multi-faceted reward model, combining interaction, estimated relevance, and structural correctness signals, is essential for effectively guiding the GFlowNet.
Adaptive trajectory sampling, which intelligently curates augmented items, significantly enhances training stability and performance compared to static or random negative sampling.
originalProblem: Standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for Generative Recommendation (GR) is 'value-unaware,' ignoring rich, multi-valued interaction signals beyond a single ground-truth item.
Existing reinforcement learning (RL) methods for GR assign rewards only at the item level, failing to provide crucial token-level supervision during the multi-step generation process of item tokens.
The misalignment between SFT's sequence-to-sequence objective and real-world GR demands limits the generation of diverse and high-quality recommendations.
results: GFlowGR consistently surpassed all tested baseline methods, including SFT and various RL-based fine-tuning approaches, across three public datasets and two GR backbones (TIGER, LETTER).
The Trajectory Balance (TB) variant of GFlowGR generally achieved more substantial performance improvements in recommendation metrics (Hit Rate, NDCG) compared to the Detailed Balance (DB) variant.
Deployed in Taobao's production system, GFlowGR demonstrated a statistically significant 0.43% relative increase in total revenue, with an even higher 1.11% lift in off-homepage search scenarios.
solution: GFlowGR leverages Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) to fine-tune LLMs for GR, formulating item generation as a multi-step process with token-level supervision.
It incorporates a comprehensive reward model that combines user interaction signals, estimated item scores from collaborative models, and partial correctness metrics (e.g., format reward).

Adaptive trajectory samplers (e.g., CM-based) construct diverse, value-aware sets of positive and augmented item trajectories, enabling the GFlowNet loss to train the LLM to generate high-reward items.
paper_summary.summary: GFlowGR, a framework from City University of Hong Kong and Alibaba Group, fine-tunes generative recommendation models by applying Generative Flow Networks to provide token-level supervision and leverage diverse user interactions. This approach addressed limitations of prior methods, leading to a 0.43% relative revenue increase in Taobao's live system.

title: GFlowGR: Fine-tuning Generative Recommendation Frameworks with Generative Flow Networks

abstract: Generative recommendation has recently emerged as a promising paradigm in sequential recommendation. It formulates the task as an autoregressive generation process, predicting discrete tokens of the next item conditioned on user interaction histories. Existing generative recommendation models are typically trained with token-level likelihood objectives, such as cross-entropy loss, while employing multi-step beam search during inference to generate ranked item candidates. However, this leads to a fundamental training-inference inconsistency: standard training assumes ground-truth history is always available, ignoring the fact that beam search prunes low-probability branches during inference. Consequently, the correct item may be prematurely discarded simply because its initial tokens (prefixes) have low scores. To address this issue, we propose the Adaptive Prefix-Aware Optimization (APAO) framework, which introduces prefix-level optimization losses to better align the training objective with the inference setting. Furthermore, we design an adaptive worst-prefix optimization strategy that dynamically focuses on the most vulnerable prefixes during training, thereby enhancing the model's ability to retain correct candidates under beam search constraints. We provide theoretical analyses to demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our framework. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets further show that APAO consistently alleviates the training-inference inconsistency and improves performance across various generative recommendation backbones. Our codes are publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The core issue in generative recommendation's training-inference gap stems from beam search's prefix-level pruning, which can prematurely discard relevant items based on their initial tokens.
Explicitly optimizing the model's ability to maintain high-scoring prefixes throughout the autoregressive generation process is critical for effective beam search inference.
The adaptive weighting strategy, by prioritizing prefixes with higher losses, allows the model to learn to be more robust against the greedy pruning nature of beam search.
originalProblem: Generative recommender systems suffer from a training-inference inconsistency: models are trained on ground-truth prefixes (teacher-forcing) but infer with beam search, which greedily prunes candidate prefixes.
This discrepancy causes relevant items to be prematurely discarded during inference if their early tokens receive low scores, despite potential global relevance.
Existing preference optimization methods for generative models primarily operate at the coarse item-level, failing to address the step-by-step prefix-level issues inherent in beam search pruning.
results: APAO consistently attained superior performance in Recall@K and NDCG@K across four public datasets and two distinct generative model architectures (TIGER and Llama).

The framework significantly outperformed existing item-level preference optimization methods, demonstrating the value of its prefix-level supervision in preventing premature item discarding during generation.

APAO achieved comparable or superior ranking quality with smaller inference beam sizes (e.g., K=20 matching K=100 baselines), indicating improved efficiency, while maintaining practical training times similar to or faster than other alignment methods.
solution: APAO, a one-stage training framework, unifies standard cross-entropy loss with a proposed prefix-aware loss.
Two variants of the prefix-aware loss were developed: a pointwise loss for token-level likelihood and a pairwise loss designed to explicitly model the ranking nature of beam search.
An adaptive worst-prefix optimization mechanism dynamically assigns higher weights to prefixes exhibiting poor performance during training, focusing learning on vulnerable intermediate steps.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University introduced the Adaptive Prefix-Aware Optimization (APAO) framework to resolve the training-inference inconsistency observed in generative recommender systems using beam search. By integrating prefix-level optimization and adaptive weighting during training, APAO ensures promising item candidates are not prematurely discarded, yielding consistent improvements in recommendation quality and efficiency across various generative backbones.

title: APAO: Adaptive Prefix-Aware Optimization for Generative Recommendation

abstract:
Contemporary recommendation systems are designed to meet users' needs by
delivering tailored lists of items that align with their specific demands or
interests. In a multi-stage recommendation system, reranking plays a crucial
role by modeling the intra-list correlations among items. The key challenge of
reranking lies in the exploration of optimal sequences within the combinatorial
space of permutations. Recent research proposes a generator-evaluator learning
paradigm, where the generator generates multiple feasible sequences and the
evaluator picks out the best sequence based on the estimated listwise score.
The generator is of vital importance, and generative models are well-suited for
the generator function. Current generative models employ an autoregressive
strategy for sequence generation. However, deploying autoregressive models in
real-time industrial systems is challenging. To address these issues, we
propose a Non-AutoRegressive generative model for reranking Recommendation
(NAR4Rec) designed to enhance efficiency and effectiveness. To tackle
challenges such as sparse training samples and dynamic candidates, we introduce
a matching model. Considering the diverse nature of user feedback, we employ a
sequence-level unlikelihood training objective to differentiate feasible
sequences from unfeasible ones. Additionally, to overcome the lack of
dependency modeling in non-autoregressive models regarding target items, we
introduce contrastive decoding to capture correlations among these items.
Extensive offline experiments validate the superior performance of NAR4Rec over
state-of-the-art reranking methods. Online A/B tests reveal that NAR4Rec
significantly enhances the user experience. Furthermore, NAR4Rec has been fully
deployed in a popular video app Kuaishou with over 300 million daily active
users.

keyInsights: Non-autoregressive generation can be successfully adapted to reranking in large-scale recommendation systems, significantly improving efficiency without sacrificing effectiveness.
Shared position embeddings and a specialized matching model are crucial for NAR models to learn robustly in environments with sparse data and dynamic item vocabularies.

A combination of unlikelihood training and contrastive losses enables NAR models to effectively capture diverse user utility and crucial intra-list item dependencies, overcoming their inherent conditional independence assumption.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) generative reranking models suffer from slow inference speed, hindering their deployment in real-time industrial recommendation systems.
AR models exhibit a training-inference discrepancy, leading to accumulated errors during sequence generation.
The left-to-right generation process of AR models limits their ability to fully utilize contextual information from succeeding items, potentially leading to suboptimal sequences.
results: Achieved approximately a 5x speedup in both training and inference time compared to autoregressive baselines in offline experiments on public and industrial datasets.
Outperformed state-of-the-art reranking baselines on both Avito (e.g., AUC of 0.7234) and Kuaishou (e.g., Recall@6 of 74.86%) datasets in offline evaluations.
Delivered significant positive improvements in online A/B tests on the Kuaishou app, including +1.161% Views, +1.71% Likes, and +2.45% Complete Views over existing production models.
solution: Developed NAR4Rec, a non-autoregressive (NAR) generative model that simultaneously predicts all items in a target recommendation sequence, drastically reducing inference time.
Designed a matching model with shared position embeddings and a candidates encoder to effectively handle sparse training data and dynamic candidate item sets.

Incorporated a multi-faceted training objective including sequence-level unlikelihood training to guide the model away from undesirable recommendations and contrastive losses to model intra-list correlations.
paper_summary.summary: Introducing NAR4Rec, a non-autoregressive generative model, facilitates real-time reranking in recommendation systems by simultaneously predicting items. This model demonstrates an approximate 5x speedup in training and inference compared to autoregressive counterparts, yielding improvements such as +1.71% in Likes and +2.45% in Complete Views during online deployment on Kuaishou.

title: Non-autoregressive Generative Models for Reranking Recommendation

abstract: Cascade Ranking is a prevalent architecture in large-scale top-k selection systems like recommendation and advertising platforms. Traditional training methods focus on single-stage optimization, neglecting interactions between stages. Recent advances have introduced interaction-aware training paradigms, but still struggle to 1) align training objectives with the goal of the entire cascade ranking (i.e., end-to-end recall of ground-truth items) and 2) learn effective collaboration patterns for different stages. To address these challenges, we propose LCRON, which introduces a novel surrogate loss function derived from the lower bound probability that ground truth items are selected by cascade ranking, ensuring alignment with the overall objective of the system. According to the properties of the derived bound, we further design an auxiliary loss for each stage to drive the reduction of this bound, leading to a more robust and effective top-k selection. LCRON enables end-to-end training of the entire cascade ranking system as a unified network. Experimental results demonstrate that LCRON achieves significant improvement over existing methods on public benchmarks and industrial applications, addressing key limitations in cascade ranking training and significantly enhancing system performance.

author_info.institution: Beijing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

keyInsights: Optimizing cascade ranking as a single, unified network directly aligns training objectives with the end-to-end recall goal, moving beyond fragmented stage-wise optimizations.
Differentiable sorting techniques enable the modeling and optimization of the full multi-stage 'survival probability' of ground-truth items through the cascade.
Combining an end-to-end collaborative loss with a robust auxiliary single-stage supervision loss is crucial for stable learning and tightening theoretical bounds in complex multi-stage systems.
originalProblem: Traditional cascade ranking systems suffer from objective misalignment, where individual stage optimizations do not directly align with the overarching end-to-end system goal.
A lack of explicit mechanisms for different cascade stages to learn effective bidirectional collaboration patterns during training, leading to suboptimal overall performance.
Previous attempts at joint optimization provided only unidirectional interactions or introduced complexity and instability.
results: LCRON significantly outperformed state-of-the-art baselines on the RecFlow public benchmark, achieving 0.8732 end-to-end Recall@10@20 (vs. 0.8678 for the next best ARF-v2).
Deployed in Kuaishou's advertising system, LCRON achieved a +4.10% increase in advertising revenue and a +1.60% increase in user conversions in a 15-day online A/B test.
The framework demonstrated robustness to hyperparameter changes, rapid convergence in streaming evaluation, and scalability to three-stage cascade systems.
solution: Proposes a novel end-to-end surrogate loss (L_e2e) that optimizes the lower bound of ground-truth item survival probability through the entire cascade using differentiable sorting.
Introduces an auxiliary single-stage loss (L_single) to tighten the theoretical bound of the end-to-end loss and provide robust supervision for individual models.
Combines the end-to-end and auxiliary losses using Uncertainty-Weighted Loss (UWL) to automatically balance their contributions and reduce hyperparameter tuning.
paper_summary.summary: LCRON introduces a unified training framework for cascade ranking systems, directly optimizing the end-to-end recall of ground-truth items by leveraging differentiable sorting and a novel surrogate loss. This approach achieved a +4.10% increase in advertising revenue and a +1.60% increase in user conversions upon deployment in Kuaishou's advertising platform.

title: Learning Cascade Ranking as One Network

abstract: Generative Sequential Recommendation (GSR) has emerged as a promising paradigm, reframing recommendation as an autoregressive sequence generation task over discrete Semantic IDs (SIDs), typically derived via codebook-based quantization. Despite its great potential in unifying retrieval and ranking, existing GSR frameworks still face two critical limitations: (1) impure and unstable semantic tokenization, where quantization methods struggle with interaction noise and codebook collapse, resulting in SIDs with ambiguous discrimination; and (2) lossy and weakly structured generation, where reliance solely on coarse-grained discrete tokens inevitably introduces information loss and neglects items' hierarchical logic. To address these issues, we propose a novel generative recommendation framework, PRISM, with Purified Representation and Integrated Semantic Modeling. Specifically, to ensure high-quality tokenization, we design a Purified Semantic Quantizer that constructs a robust codebook via adaptive collaborative denoising and hierarchical semantic anchoring mechanisms. To compensate for information loss during quantization, we further propose an Integrated Semantic Recommender, which incorporates a dynamic semantic integration mechanism to integrate fine-grained semantics and enforces logical validity through a semantic structure alignment objective. PRISM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across four real-world datasets, demonstrating substantial performance gains, particularly in high-sparsity scenarios.

keyInsights: Denoising collaborative signals and enforcing hierarchical semantic structure are critical for learning robust, discriminative, and uniformly utilized codebooks for Semantic IDs.
Dynamically integrating continuous semantic features into the generation process effectively mitigates information loss inherent in discrete tokenization, enhancing fine-grained item distinction.
Explicitly aligning generated SIDs with their hierarchical structure and adapting decoding temperature to branching density significantly improves semantic consistency and recommendation precision.
originalProblem: Existing lightweight generative sequential recommenders (GSRs) struggle with impure and unstable semantic tokenization, leading to ambiguous and non-discriminative Semantic IDs (SIDs).
These systems suffer from information loss because they discard fine-grained continuous features after quantization into discrete SIDs.
Current generative methods often treat hierarchical SIDs as flat sequences, neglecting their intrinsic structural logic and leading to semantically deviant recommendations.
results: PRISM achieved state-of-the-art performance, outperforming all baselines in 15 out of 16 cases across four Amazon datasets, including a 33.9% Recall@10 improvement over a strong generative baseline (TIGER) on the sparse 'CDs' dataset.

The framework demonstrated exceptional robustness to data sparsity, showing substantial improvements in recommending medium and long-tail items, and achieved near-optimal codebook perplexity (248.5) with a low collision rate (1.79%).
PRISM maintained efficiency with a compact parameter size (5.5M) and the lowest inference latency (29.1ms) among generative methods, making it practical for real-world deployment.
solution: A Purified Semantic Quantizer component constructs high-quality and stable SIDs by employing Adaptive Collaborative Denoising and Hierarchical Semantic Anchoring to mitigate noise and codebook collapse.
An Integrated Semantic Recommender compensates for quantization loss by dynamically integrating continuous item-level features via Mixture-of-Experts during autoregressive generation.
Semantic Structure Alignment and Adaptive Temperature Scaling are applied during generation to enforce hierarchical consistency and calibrate uncertainty based on decoding path density.
paper_summary.summary: PRISM is a framework for generative sequential recommendation that constructs purified semantic item representations and integrates fine-grained semantic modeling during autoregressive generation. The approach significantly improves recommendation accuracy and robustness, especially for sparse data, while maintaining low inference latency.

title: PRISM: Purified Representation and Integrated Semantic Modeling for Generative Sequential Recommendation

abstract: Re-ranking draws increased attention on both academics and industries, which rearranges the ranking list by modeling the mutual influence among items to better meet users' demands. Many existing re-ranking methods directly take the initial ranking list as input, and generate the optimal permutation through a well-designed context-wise model, which brings the evaluation-before-reranking problem. Meanwhile, evaluating all candidate permutations brings unacceptable computational costs in practice. Thus, to better balance efficiency and effectiveness, online systems usually use a two-stage architecture which uses some heuristic methods such as beam-search to generate a suitable amount of candidate permutations firstly, which are then fed into the evaluation model to get the optimal permutation. However, existing methods in both stages can be improved through the following aspects. As for generation stage, heuristic methods only use point-wise prediction scores and lack an effective judgment. As for evaluation stage, most existing context-wise evaluation models only consider the item context and lack more fine-grained feature context modeling. This paper presents a novel end-to-end re-ranking framework named PIER to tackle the above challenges which still follows the two-stage architecture and contains two mainly modules named FPSM and OCPM. We apply SimHash in FPSM to select top-K candidates from the full permutation based on user's permutation-level interest in an efficient way. Then we design a novel omnidirectional attention mechanism in OCPM to capture the context information in the permutation. Finally, we jointly train these two modules end-to-end by introducing a comparative learning loss. Offline experiment results demonstrate that PIER outperforms baseline models on both public and industrial datasets, and we have successfully deployed PIER on Meituan food delivery platform.

keyInsights: Considering user interest at the permutation level, rather than just item-level interest, allows for more effective personalization in re-ranking.
Efficiently selecting promising permutations from a large search space can be achieved by leveraging SimHash techniques to match historical user behaviors with candidate permutations.
A fine-grained, omnidirectional attention mechanism can effectively model both inter-item and intra-feature relationships within a permutation to predict user interaction.
originalProblem: Traditional ranking models score items independently, neglecting interdependencies and context among items in a list, which can lead to sub-optimal user experience.
Current re-ranking approaches often suffer from the 'evaluation-before-re-ranking' problem, where initial ranking bias influences subsequent re-ranking decisions.
Existing methods face challenges in efficiently exploring the vast permutation space and lack comprehensive fine-grained feature context modeling for user interests.
results: PIER outperformed baseline models on both public (Avito) and industrial (Meituan) datasets in offline experiments, showing improved AUC, LogLoss, and HR@1 metrics.
Online A/B testing on Meituan's food delivery platform demonstrated that PIER achieved an uplift in both Click-Through Rate (CTR) and Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV) compared to the existing strategy.
The framework maintained acceptable inference times, confirming its suitability for real-time deployment in large-scale industrial settings.
solution: Introduces PIER, a two-stage end-to-end trainable framework consisting of a Fine-grained Permutation Selection Module (FPSM) and an Omnidirectional Context-aware Prediction Module (OCPM).
FPSM utilizes SimHash and time-aware weighting to efficiently select top-K permutations by matching them with user's historical click behaviors at a permutation level.

OCPM employs an Omnidirectional Attention Unit (OAU) and Target Attention Unit (TAU) to predict click-through rates by modeling comprehensive item and fine-grained feature context within permutations.
paper_summary.summary: The PIER framework addresses re-ranking limitations in e-commerce by introducing a two-stage architecture that models user interests at a permutation level. This approach improved Click-Through Rate and Gross Merchandise Volume in real-world deployment on Meituan's food delivery platform.

title: PIER: Permutation-Level Interest-Based End-to-End Re-ranking Framework in E-commerce

abstract: Mainstream ranking approaches typically follow a Generator-Evaluator two-stage paradigm, where a generator produces candidate lists and an evaluator selects the best one. Recent work has attempted to enhance performance by expanding the number of candidate lists, for example, through multi-generator settings. However, ranking involves selecting a recommendation list from a combinatorially large space. Simply enlarging the candidate set remains ineffective, and performance gains quickly saturate. At the same time, recent advances in large recommendation models have shown that end-to-end one-stage models can achieve promising performance with the expectation of scaling laws. Motivated by this, we revisit ranking from a generator-only one-stage perspective. We theoretically prove that, for any (finite Multi-)Generator-Evaluator model, there always exists a generator-only model that achieves strictly smaller approximation error to the optimal ranking policy, while also enjoying scaling laws as its size increases. Building on this result, we derive an evidence upper bound of the one-stage optimization objective, from which we find that one can leverage a reward model trained on real user feedback to construct a reference policy in a group-relative manner. This reference policy serves as a practical surrogate of the optimal policy, enabling effective training of a large generator-only ranker. Based on these insights, we propose GoalRank, a generator-only ranking framework. Extensive offline experiments on public benchmarks and large-scale online A/B tests demonstrate that GoalRank consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
title: GoalRank: Group-Relative Optimization for a Large Ranking Model

abstract: Recommender systems in multi-behavior domains, such as advertising and e-commerce, aim to guide users toward high-value but inherently sparse conversions. Leveraging auxiliary behaviors (e.g., clicks, likes, shares) is therefore essential. Recent progress on generative recommendations has brought new possibilities for multi-behavior sequential recommendation. However, existing generative approaches face two significant challenges: 1) Inadequate Sequence Modeling: capture the complex, cross-level dependencies within user behavior sequences, and 2) Lack of Suitable Datasets: publicly available multi-behavior recommendation datasets are almost exclusively derived from e-commerce platforms, limiting the validation of feasibility in other domains, while also lacking sufficient side information for semantic ID generation. To address these issues, we propose a novel generative framework, GAMER (Generative Augmentation and Multi-lEvel behavior modeling for Recommendation), built upon a decoder-only backbone. GAMER introduces a cross-level interaction layer to capture hierarchical dependencies among behaviors and a sequential augmentation strategy that enhances robustness in training. To further advance this direction, we collect and release ShortVideoAD, a large-scale multi-behavior dataset from a mainstream short-video platform, which differs fundamentally from existing e-commerce datasets and provides pretrained semantic IDs for research on generative methods. Extensive experiments show that GAMER consistently outperforms both discriminative and generative baselines across multiple metrics.
title: Generative Sequential Recommendation via Hierarchical Behavior Modeling
